Multiple companies for whom she consulted mentioned that they had to send newly hired statistics and data science majors to workshops in order to enhance their communications and teamwork skills. To that end she proposed a model for helping the students who are enrolled in the capstone course to be exposed to constructs that underlie what is referred to as EQ (emotional intelligence) or soft skills in the world of today. They expressed that these soft skills were essential for successful team work and elaboration of the statistical findings for consumers of statistics. Statistics as a multidisciplinary subject and it is used in every discipline (quantitative and non-quantitative) to demystify the patterns in the small and large data sets collected in different disciplines.
A schematic of this model is presented in chapter one. Once Esfandiari, Lew, and Zes who teach “Introduction to Statistical Consulting” and have also done consulting in various industries, agreed on the structure of the model proposed by Esfandiari, the next step was to write the relevant chapters that introduced the readers to the definition of the underlying constructs, as well as coming up with an approach to measure them.
In the next step (Esfandiari, Lew, and Zes) wrote a number of chapters along with exercises (workshops), that provide the readers with the opportunity to learn about the definition of the constructs underlying EQ in data science. Additionally, after a thorough search of the literature and relevant media, they created surveys in the form of Likert scales.
The dimensions of the underlying model include “active listening”, “communication”, “conflict resolution”, “empathy”, “flexibility”, “locus of control”, and “team building”. These concepts are discussed in chapters two to seven.
In Statistics 140XP, students are initially instructed to practice communication skills and listening skills in two formats (a) individually, by recording a personal introduction and revising it for the purpose of sharing with others and (b) in pairs through a randomized meet and greet exercise (see the appendix/Chapter for the exercise). At the completion of this set of exercises and evaluations, students begin initial forays into team building via a set of exercises.
Team building in a multicultural setting has its challenges but the process of resolving these are uniquely satisfying to our students. We start by showing students a successful end result - a team presenting findings to a client and fielding a set of clarifying questions from the client in a follow-up. From that, we build out towards that end goal along the way working through skills such as active listening, flexibility, empathy.
As an illustration, approximately midway through the term, students are randomized into teams and asked to design a data dashboard with the goal of communicating the relationships in the data to a client with minimal computer literacy (e.g., able to use a portable device or desktop computer with a high level interface such as Windows). The end result is presented and open to critique by their peers and supervising faculty. Successful teams demonstrate that they are able to work as a cohesive unit and develop a dashboard from initial sketches to working model in the specified time frame. These teams have also practiced communication, listening, empathy, flexibility and conflict resolution in the process.
Based on Esfandiari’s extensive experience teaching applied statistics, writing about applied statistics, and consulting in the evaluation of program/interventions in industry, education, entrepreneurship, medicine, and public health, etc. as well as her participation in a seven day workshop on “Creative Leadership” that involved answering major battery of scales that related to creative leadership , she proposed a model that elaborates the underlying constructs that help to introduce and educate the students with respective to those components of emotional intelligence that are needed to succeed as a data scientist or anybody who aspires to succeed in working in a collaborative and yet competitive environment.
After discussing this model with (Lew, and Zes) who teach Stat 140XP, we all agreed on the relevant components and the next step was how we should go about evaluating the extent to which our students have developed this attribute. The best model that we could follow was a pre and post design. Ethically speaking we needed to give all the students to take the survey. Therefore, we could not follow a model like random assignment of some students to take the survey and others did not take it.
In the next step, based on the review of the relevant literature, we developed the relevant scales and after careful revision of the scales developed, an online survey was created. The items on the survey are in the form of a Likert scale (one to five). However, in order to allow the students to choose answers that could reflect decimals (like 3.5, or 4.4 etc.), Zes developed an algorithm that made this possible.
This model is discussed in chapter one of this book and it involves six components including active listening , communication, empathy, developing an internal locus of control (learning to associate their success and failure to their own efforts), conflict resolution, empathy, and team building. With respect to data management and visualization, we teach the techniques of creating clean and correct data, and creation of visualization tools such as dashboards, web APPs, and other related skills.
In order to evaluate the extent to which the students have improved in the EQ aspect of the course,we (Esfandiari, Lew, and Zes) have developed a bank of surveys that measure the attitude of the students toward the six mentioned components and created an online survey that the students take prior to registration is Stat 140XP and after finishing 141XP. Sample results are provided in the appendix.
In addition to the pre and post data on the six components of EQ, the survey also contains many questions about the demographic background of the students. In the future courses, we will be able to share the findings with the students enrolled in these classes and also use this data in teaching the theoretical part of 141XP. Research has shown that the students find the data that is about them very interesting. Therefore, we plan to use this data as a teaching tool.
IN 141XP:
We teach the knowledge of new statistical methods that are not discussed in other courses and have them practice writing the results found within context and with plots that are understandable by a non-statistical audience. These topics are selected based on the projects that have been presented to us by the clients over the last two decades. Example topics are: random intercept models, generalizability theory, creation of valid and reliable instruments for attitude measurement, and in-depth analysis of classification methods used in medical research including concepts like ROC curves which we do not discuss in our courses.
Through the activities elaborated above, we ascertain that our students are trained to engage in appropriate statistical practice and comply with these guidelines and advocate for a culture that supports ethical statistical practice .
Promote a respectful, safe, and productive work environment. Encourage constructive engagement to improve statistical practice.
In both Stats 140 and 141XP, a safe and comfortable working environment is created for the teams to work on the projects assigned to them. Due to their training in the EQ of data science, the teams have been collaborative and generated professional reports as well as presentations to the client. Conflicts have arisen on rare occasions between team members. But, due to their training in constructive techniques of conflict management, the team members have learned how to resolve their disagreements such as one member contributing more to the project or not including everyone in group emails, or few members taking the active role and leaving others out, or some members not showing up to the team meetings by talking to each other, or bringing it up with the instructors.
Once we are presented with a situation like this our strategy with dealing it is as follows: 1) we talk to each team member individually, 2) we have a meeting with the whole team and allow each member to explain their position, and 3) we have them agree on a strategy that is agreeable with everyone.
In rare circumstances, we have had to split large teams into two because of different interests and different skills in programming and knowledge of statistics.
For example( Esfandiari)in a project one team was really interested in analyzing president Trump’s comments on calling the corona virus and “Chinese Virus”. This was during the time of the pandemic when teaching was online and it was hard to find actual clients to work with student teams. Half of the group members were very excited about the project and quickly scraped all the verbal comments by Presenting Trump from the Internet and started using methods like NLP (natural language processing) as well as mathematical modeling of the relevant data. This part of the team was complaining that the other members are not paying attention and participating in the work. Whereas, the other half were complaining that they are not included and do not feel they belong to this team. After talking to all members individually and talking to them as a whole group (Esfandiari), it was concluded that the members who felt excluded just did not have the same skill set as those that were using NLP and mathematical modeling of the data. While in the group meeting they were verbalizing that they will try to collaborate and make this work, it was concluded that this collaboration could not be very constructive due to the difference in knowledge, interest, and skill set of the two parts of the team. Thus, it was concluded to allow those that had actively worked on the project move forward with their part and the other half of the team chose another project to work on. They were given some extra time to finish and both teams ended up creating very thorough and comprehensive final reports.
The conclusion that we can reach from experiences such as the above is that training in EQ and in skills like conflict resolution does help the teams to settle their disagreements in most of the situations. However, in some circumstances, the instructor needs to intervene by: 1) listening to each team member, 2) having the whole team meet and allowing each one to express their viewpoint, and 3) coming up with a solution to resolve the dilemmas at hand in a way that is best for everyone. In some circumstances it might help to ask another colleague to attend the final meeting in order to get another viewpoint.

Identify and/or create opportunities for team members/mentees to develop professionally and maintain their proficiency.

In 141XP (Esfandiari) we stop lecturing on Statistics in week five. However, attendance is required in both lecture and section and the teams meet to work on their projecst. This strategy has been very successful and the team members do not need to find time outside class to work on the project. Thus, they will have 15-20 hours in which they can meet with each other to work on the project while the instructor meets with each group, monitors the progress of their work, and answer any questions they might, The work on the final project is completed using the milestones elaborated below:
Milestone one: Develop a clear and concise understanding of the client’s question
The teams meet with the client in week five. The client presents the team with the data set, the codebook, and question to be answered. If possible, this meeting is held in person and if not then using Zoom because some of our clients are not local. The meetingt lasts about 30 minutes to one hour. During that time, we try to ascertain that the team has a comprehensive and accurate understanding of the problem to be solved. We do this by: 1) Making sure that the team has a full understanding of the predictors and the outcome involved in the question asked. 2) Identify how the variables are measured. For example, which variables are numerical, which ones are categorical with how many levels as well as the definition of each level. If a numerical variable has been transformed to categorical, they need to know how it was done, why it was done the way it was, and what each level means. For example, BMI (Body Mass Index) is a numerical variable. In medical data this variable can be treated as categorical (low BMI <18.5), (average BM 18.5 -25), (BMI indicating being overweight 25-30), and (BMI indicating obesity>30). Once the team has a thorough understanding of the question to be asked, we have them re-write it in their own words and read it back to the client. The reason that we spend so much time on milestone one is because experience has shown us that if the teams do not have an accurate and complete understanding of the question and the relevant variables, this would become a major source of error and bias throughout the whole process.
Milestone two. During week eight, the team presents the client with …

A schematic or road map like the following example which shows the relationship between the outcome of interest as well as the predictors. This has proved to be very helpful in helping the team see the big picture and create a foundation for EDA and the next steps in the final report.

In the following example, (Esfandiari), we are using a schematic from an article3 to show a categorical outcome with four levels including no depression or chronic pain. The other three levels include depression, chronic pain or both. There are four hypotheses that also clarify the predictors are of interest:

Is low childhood socio-economic status related to depression, pain or both in adulthood.
Is maternal depression related to the depression, pain or both in adulthood.
Is adulthood socio-economic status related to the depression, pain or both in adulthood.
Does the effect of low childhood SES on adulthood depression, pain, or both vary with age (Interaction effect)


2. A comprehensive Exploratory Data Analysis

A complete EDA (Exploratory data analysis) which involves histogram, frequency tables, contingency tables, side-by-side box plots, correlation matrices, etc. (Esfandiari) Experience has shown that EDA is the most important step in a project, It is during this step that we discover if histograms are skewed and need transformation, this is when we come across situation that none of the transformations used help to create normality in the numerical variables. The next thing we might think of is cutting the numerical variable into categories.
As statisticians we tend to create the cuts that look best from the statistical point of view such as cutting at the median or every quartile to create similar frequencies in the different categories. While after talking to the client, you are instructed that the cut needs to be made at a specific point. This happens very often in medical research where the client would tell you the cut needs to be at a specific number like 4.1 millimeter or a blood pressure of 180/90. This has been very educational for the teams because they learn the importance of communication and involving the client when they are making transformation to the variables.
Another important thing that has happened very often is creation of contingency tables to show the relationship between categorical variables. In many circumstances, the frequency tables generated do not have similar or close numbers in the different cells. Therefore, the application of standard machine learning methods such as logistic for multinomial regression leads to range results in P-value. This is a result of very low cell frequency and the assumptions of the model are not met. In such circumstances one should either employ methodologies designed for rare events or utilize non-parametric statistical methods.
Non-parametric statistical methods are not part of our undergraduate curriculum. However, since they frequently arise in studies where data is hard to collect, we discuss them. For example, experience has shown us that sometimes three different hospitals must collaborate to create a data set that shows a rare surgery for 40 patients. These experiences have taught the teams the importance of EDA and data cleaning. Based on the talk by one of our prior students with five years of experience in the industry, he mentioned that I spent 60% of my time on data cleaning and doing EDA.
These experiences have taught our students that the worst practice for a data scientist would be to try to match a statistical method to the problem presented and start running the model and creating the outcome. What happens is that they end up creating lengthy outputs which they do not know how to interpret.
For example, on the research on areas like campus climate and diversity when I have asked the students about their ethnic background, and suppose we get ten different answers. Another question is whether our campus climate is very comfortable, comfortable, somewhat comfortable, uncomfortable, or very uncomfortable. Suppose the research question of interest is:
Does the effect of comfort in our climate on the sense of belonging to our campus (numerical) vary with ethnicity?
The answer requires us to study the interaction between ethnicity and comfort level. This would create a ten by five contingency table with varying frequencies and sparsity in some cells. Once the regression model is run, it will create many meaningless interaction effects with strange p-values. Then the challenge is: What does this weird model mean and how do I interpret it?
However, EDA would help to pool together certain categories on campus climate and ethnicity and a contingency table with reasonable frequencies and then the created model would make sense, the coefficients would have higher reliability/consistency, and the data would be interpretable. Exposure to such practices helps the teams experience first hand the importance of the reasonable number of observations on the reliability, and interpretability of the coefficients of the model as well as its reproducibility.

3. Decision about what method they are going to use to analyze the data, a sample printout with relevant plots, as well as the interpretation of relevant findings within context.
The third step (Esfandiari) in milestone two is to decide what statistical method the team is going to use to analyze the data to answer the client’s question. In this step the team presents the clients with a number of professionally prepared tables and plots that shows the EDA performed.
The teams are advised not to cut and paste computer output from R or Python to their presentation to the client and the final report.. Rather, they are trained to present their findings in tables and plots presented in the refereed journal articles that are used as a major component of teaching the theoretical part of 141XP. (Esfandiari). These articles are also used as a means of enhancing statistical literacy and teaching the students how to write professional abstracts, publishable articles, with major emphasis on interpretation of statistical findings, tables, and plots within the context of the problem to be solved.
For example, if they are using logistic regression to analyze their data, they are required to create a table representing odds, 95% confidence interval for the odds, plot of odds, R curve, and other indices of accuracy such as confusion matrix, etc.
4. Q and A between the team and the client
In the fourth and final step of milestone two: 1) The client will ask their questions about the results that were presented, 2) suggest the revisions that are needed and the additional analyses or plots that they think needs to be created to answer their questions, 3) the team members are also given a chance to ask clients about any questions they might have or any specific points they are confused about. The typical questions that the teams have generally relates to coding of the data, missing data and if they use imputation techniques, definition of variables, levels of categorical variables, how they could potentially recode or relevel the categorical variables, if they could cut the numerical variables to categories and at what points these cuts should happen . These types of questions come up more often in projects where the team is unfamiliar with the content. Best examples are medical projects. In instances like this the teams are asked to check the definition of unfamiliar terms in the literature and learn about them. Lack of familiarity with the content of the question to be answered is very important. In many instances data scientists and statisticians consult on areas in which they have no familiarity.
In my own personal experience on consulting (Esfandiari), I ended up consulting with a major foundation whose mission was designing and evaluation of interventions that taught students from elementary to college level how to write a business plan and create their own business.
One of the major components of this program was teaching basic knowledge of economic and entrepreneurship concepts. The other component was ascertaining that the intervention helped the students develop a positive attitude about entrepreneurship, developed the relevant skills, and learned about the ethics of business and ethical behaviors that an entrepreneur is expected to exhibit.
My training in experimental design gave me the expertise to design the relevant interventions in the schools. However, I needed to acquire the relevant knowledge and skills that required me to develop the relevant tests and attitude scales that were necessary for the evaluation of the interventions. This is what happens in Stat 141XP at a much smaller scale. The teams have clients from all walks of life including eye surgery, internal medicine, analysis of vocal data, analysis of data resulting from psychiatric scales, civic education, psychology etc. The experience of working with a client and in content areas with which they are unfamiliar helps to prepare them for the jobs that they will get after they graduate from college. They will end up working in business, movie industry, music, sports, non-profit organizations, biostatistics, etc. Thus, they realize that having a thorough understanding of the client’s question requires familiarity and knowledge of the relevant context.
One of my interesting experiences (Esfandiari) was trying to create a scale for measuring ethical behavior in elementary school students. After a major search of the literature and talking to some experts, we concluded that elementary school children would have a very hard time responding to a Likert Scale on business. The other thing that I learned from the search of literature on ethics was that it is kind of difficult to make ethical behavior “black” and “white” and it could potentially depend on the situation. I remember reading about this scenario in which somebody’s loved one had cancer and the medicine cost 2000 dollars and they had no money. One of the options was to let the individual die and the other was to steal money for the drug.
After extensive research and given the age of elementary children who are not yet 14 and based on theories of cognitive science (Jean Piaget), cannot yet engage in “abstract thinking” or “hypothetical deductive thinking”, I decided to present the students with simple scenarios to which they related and created three types of responses including ethical, partially ethical, and unethical. One sample scenario was as follows:
Janice has five red pens, and her friend Mary is unhappy because she has no red pens. What do you think her friend should do because she needs a red pen to complete her drawings.

Borrow one of the red pens and return it so Janice would not even notice it.
Take one because Janice will still be left with four which is more than enough
Tell her parents that she needs a red pen and ask them to get her one

This type of scale resonated with elementary students because the scenarios were simple and they described situations with which they were familiar.
The conclusion that we want to reach is that statistics and data science is a multidisciplinary subject and it is used in every field to answer the questions of interest. Therefore, the individuals trained in this area not only should have the readiness to learn new computer languages and visualization skills, they should also be aware that familiarity with the knowledge of the area in which they are consulting is a necessary step in their success as a statistical consultant, advisor, or practitioner.

Continued communication and collaboration with the client throughout the whole project

Of course the interaction between the client and the teams is ongoing and usually either the client or somebody in his/her team will be available to answer our questions from the inception to the completion of the project. For example, in our projects with Jules Stein (Esfandiari), the professor who has been one of our main clients ( Daniel Rootman, MD, MPH), assigns one of his fellows to each project.
Fellows are individuals who have finished their MD and residency training in their relevant specialization, in this case ophthalmology, and are working for a year or more with a research professor such as Dr. Rootman and their major objective is to collaborate on ongoing research projects, and help with publishing of the research results in the refereed journal articles in their fields.
Fellows are in contact with us throughout the project and answer any question we might have about data cleaning, outliers, definition of medical terms, advising on cutoff points for changing numerical to categorical variables in case of dealing with very skewed distributions, whether the teams are on the right track with the type of analysis they are conducting etc. Having access to these experts makes our collaboration very smooth and also shows the team members the importance of collaboration and communication throughout the different phases of the project.
Another example of our collaboration (Esfandiari) is the work we do with the Chancellor’s Office at UCLA. The Executive Director of the Chancellor’s Office of Data Analytics Office of Academic Planning and Budget. The Executive Director of this office is our own PhD graduate, Dr. Adam Sugano and his colleague Dr. Cecilia Zhang, Associate Director of Data Science, have done an incredible job of supporting the 141XP class by providing the teams with extensive data sets, codebooks, as well as advising them on many consulting projects that relate to data collected on UCLA students, faculty, course work, and how we can improve things. Through collaboration with graduates of our own department the students can get to see the importance of continued contact and collaboration with colleagues and the importance of “giving back to the community”. (Esfandiari).
Examples of sample projects include development of dashboards that help the students in the process of enrollment. Sometimes a required course that the students want to enroll in gets filled up in their department. The objective of the developed dashboard is to help them find a similar course from another department that fulfills the requirement and is accepted by their home department. These dashboards were developed based on the course description provided to the students. The outcome was quite acceptable with reasonable accuracy.
Another project done by our teams (Esfandiari) was a student created ranking of the US colleges and universities and comparing their rankings with the rankings created by US News and World Reports. The findings indicated that the rankings were very similar for the top and bottom ten percent but different for the middle 80%. One of the goals of the project was to evaluate the criteria of what is considered the best college. For instance, one criteria might be that the best college is the most selective. While this may not be the best criteria. The best criteria may be the college that helps students from different backgrounds succeed and graduate.
All of the studies done for Dr Sugano and Zhang involve complicated methods such as web scraping, combining data from different resources, cleaning the data, preparing an appropriate codebook, extensive exploratory data analysis, and using the proper machine learning techniques for doing the relevant predictions and forecasting. The teams probably spend 60% of their time on preparation of the data sets and these are the scales that they are taught in Stat 140XP by Lew and Zes.
Statistics 140 and 141XP complement each other and the set of skills and attitudes that the students acquire in 140XP (which we emphasize EQ in Data Science) is a major prerequisite to the success of a team in working with real clients and solving real world problems in Stat 141XP.

Abstract

During the final presentation, the client comments on the definition of variables, EDA, statistical tables, plots, and interpretation of findings. They suggest what revisions need to be made and what additional analysis or tables need to be included.
Later the team incorporates the client’s suggestions and recommendations and prepares the final report.
One of the most useful parts of the final reports are suggestions for further steps that need to be taken. This is very educational both from the teaching and project point of view in 141XP (Esfandiari). These reports are used as teaching tools. During lecture or as homework the students analyze these reports, get familiar with the limitations, and are required to come up with suggestions for methods that could be used to answer further questions that the client wants answered but the prior team had no time to complete.
This is an important step because it creates some kind of continuity in our collaboration with the client (Esfandiari). When we go back to talk to the client about giving our teams new projects, the client is highly likely to want to continue working on some of the prior projects. This particularly happens with very small data sets. I recall (Esfandiari) that in one project related to examining the effect of a new drug on some kind of eye disorder there were four hospitals that collaborated on this study. As the study progressed the four hospitals were able to add to the data set and therefore teams were able to use machine learning methods creating models with more accuracy and less problem of overfitting.
Based on my extensive experience (Esfandiari) overfitting seems to be one of the major problems that medical interventions related to rare disease face. The sample size is very small and yet there are many demographic variables and comorbidities (the simultaneous presence of multiple medical conditions that relate to the outcome of interest) that the researcher is interested in. Therefore, we are faced with too many predictors and a small sample size. This creates not only overfitting but poor practice of statistics.

Advocate for appropriate, timely, inclusion and participation of statistical practitioners as contributors/collaborators. Promote appropriate recognition of the contributions of statistical practitioners, including authorship if applicable.

Establish a culture that values validation of assumptions and assessment of model/algorithm performance over time and across relevant subgroups, as needed. Communicate with relevant stakeholders regarding model or algorithm maintenance, failure, or actual or proposed modifications.

Knows the definitions of and procedures relating to misconduct in their institutional setting. Seeks to clarify facts and intent before alleging misconduct by others. Recognizes that differences of opinion and honest error do not constitute unethical behavior.
Avoids condoning or appearing to condone statistical, scientific, or professional misconduct. Encourages other practitioners to avoid misconduct or the appearance of misconduct.
Does not make allegations that are poorly founded or intended to intimidate. Recognizes such allegations as potential ethics violations.
Lodges complaints of misconduct discreetly and to the relevant institutional body.


Does not act on allegations of misconduct without appropriate institutional referral, including those allegations originating from social media accounts or email listservs.


Insists upon a transparent and fair process to adjudicate claims of misconduct. Maintains confidentiality when participating in an investigation. Discloses the investigation results honestly to appropriate parties and stakeholders once they are available.
Refuses to publicly question or discredit the reputation of a person based on a specific accusation of misconduct while due process continues to unfold.
Following an investigation of misconduct, supports the efforts of all parties involved to resume their careers in as normal a manner as possible, consistent with the outcome of the investigation.
Avoids and acts to discourage retaliation against or damage to the employability of those who responsibly call attention to possible misconduct.

title: 1 Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
Professional integrity and accountability require taking responsibility for one’s work. Ethical statistical practice supports valid and prudent decision-making with appropriate methodology. The ethical statistical practitioner represents their capabilities and activities honestly and treats others with respect.
The ethical statistical practitioner:

Takes responsibility for evaluating potential tasks, assessing whether they have (or can attain) sufficient competence to execute each task and that the work and timeline are feasible. Does not solicit or deliver work for which they are not qualified or that they would not be willing to have peer reviewed.

Example. Suppose that a department of statistics and data science offers a consulting course in which students act as statistical consultants and undertake projects that are brought in by different clients. If the instructor is not certain that the team assigned to the project does not have the competence or training to execute the relevant task within the required time frame, this project should not be accepted. This becomes even more problematic if the client intends to publish the results of the project is a peer-reviewed journal. However, if the instructor makes it clear from the beginning that the students’ work will be exploratory, and they will not necessarily be able to answer the question of interest, and the client accepts that, one could go forward with that type of a project.
In many consulting projects, if the consultants believe that they do not have the training to execute the project, they could always seek the advice of an external expert and compensate them accordingly.

Uses methodology and data that are valid, relevant, and appropriate, without favoritism or prejudice, and in a manner intended to produce valid, interpretable, and reproducible results.

Example of validity
The purpose of a study was to find out the attitude of Muslim women residing in southern California toward doing mammography as a means of breast cancer prevention as a function of their knowledge of breast cancer. In order to develop a valid survey for the participants’ knowledge of breast cancer, content validity needed to be ascertained by having a few breast cancer specialists examine the survey and make sure the questions asked reflect a valid knowledge of breast cancer.
Example of interpretable results
One of the members on the board wanted to explain the results of the breast cancer study on mass media so that it could be followed by non-statisticians. Logistic regression was used to examine the likelihood of doing a mammography as a function of multiple factors such as knowledge of breast cancer, cultural background, level of education etc. Simple odds ratios within context with no mention of the technicalities were used to explain the findings to the board member.
Example of lack of prejudice
In order to learn more about the beliefs of the Muslim women residing in southern California toward pre-screening for breast cancer, one-on-one interviews were done. The results were so different depending on the country of origin and spoken language. Some women said I must have done something bad and now I am being punished. Others said I do not want anybody to know because my friends will leave me. Others would say, I want to let everyone know and this is something random that happened to me. These one-on-one interviews helped us in understanding and appreciating the differences in attitudes of women from different backgrounds toward pre-screening for breast cancer and how they felt after they had the cancer . These experiences are very helpful in developing lack of prejudice and helping in future projects that a consultant could encounter.
Example of reproducible results
Reproducible results are a lot more possible in conditions where the factors affecting the outcome can be controlled. For example, if one wanted to examine the effect of light, a fertilizer, and water on the growth of tomatoes, as long as one chooses plots of land that were similar in terms of light and irrigation and controlled the concentration of the fertilizer, then the results are much more likely to be reproducible. However, interventions that involve multiple confounding factors that are hard to control, are less likely to produce similar results.
However, one can always make an effort to minimize the effect of confounders. Examples include:

Random assignment of participants to control and experimental groups. Random assignment is assumed to establish equivalency between the treatment and control group.
Making equivalent groups based on some confounding factor. For instance, if weight or BMI is a confounding factor in a medical intervention, one could rank the participants from the lowest to the height BMI, then make pairs, and assign one member of each pair to the control and the other to the experimental group.
Using confounding factors such as prior knowledge as covariates in educational interventions, and
Using background variables such as gender, age, ethnicity, and comorbidities such as hypertension, heart issues, diabetic, etc. are used as covariates in medical research.
Using a combination of the above; namely random assignment plus covariates is another solution


Does not knowingly conduct statistical practices that exploit vulnerable populations or create or perpetuate unfair outcomes.

The tragic tale of the Tuskegee Syphilis Study serves as an illustration1. From 1932 to 1972, the U.S. Public Health Service conducted a study on the progression of untreated syphilis in African American men in Macon County, Alabama. The participants were not informed of their diagnosis, not given informed consent, and were not provided with effective treatment even after penicillin was recognized as the standard of care for syphilis. The statistical data gathered from this unethical experiment was used in medical literature for many years, highlighting a significant abuse of trust and misuse of research ethics.

Opposes efforts to predetermine or influence the results of statistical practices and resists pressure to selectively interpret data.

Lack of understanding between practical and statistical significance is frequently a source of pressure from the client with respect to interpretation of the findings.
Example. No matter what statistical method is used, if the sample size is large enough, the standard error will decrease resulting in high t or F values and p-values less than 0.05, indicating statistical significance. Whereas, with large enough sample sizes, p-values less than 0.05 are highly likely to happen with low effect size, example lack of practical difference between the mean of the experimental and control group along with low power, or negligible R-squared in regression analysis, or very low accuracy in machine learning models such as logistic or ordinal regression.
Therefore, in situations with very large sample sizes leading to statistical but no practical significance, the statistical consultant needs to explain to the client that p-value smaller than 0.05 are necessary but not sufficient for reporting major findings and potentially asking for further funding.

Accepts full responsibility for their own work, does not take credit for the work of others, and gives credit to those who contribute. Respects and acknowledges the intellectual property of others.

Statistical consultation is not a solo process and always involves collaboration between a team of people. This was illustrated in the chapters that explain the multiple dimensions involved in successful team work including active listening, communication, having an internal locus of control, conflict resolution, empathy and team building. Thus, when a statistical consultant is asked to take the major role in designing a study, advise on development of instruments, data collection, analysis, and reporting of the data, the consultant should have respect for the work of all members and endorse them as needed.

Strives to follow, and encourages all collaborators to follow, an established protocol for authorship. Advocates for recognition commensurate with each person’s contribution to the work. Recognizes that inclusion as an author does imply, while acknowledgement may imply, endorsement of the work.

When it comes to publishing the work in refereed journal articles, the statistician along with the consultant need to: 1) ascertain that the paper is written along with the guideline and standard of the journal of interest, 2) decide on the order of authorship based on the contributions of each team member.
What has to be pointed out is that the level of teaching and leadership that the statistical consultant is responsible for varies with the project. If the consultant is collaborating with researchers who are well known in the field, have an established theory, and want to find out the extent to which the data collected by them supports the hypotheses they want to prove, the role of the statistical consultant may be limited to advising the client on the right statistical method, overseeing analysis of the data, ascertaining that the relevant assumptions are met, and explaining the results in a language that is clear and understandable to the consumers of that research.
However, in some other projects, the statistical consultant is in the scene from the inception to the completion of the project. He may be asked to answer a question about a topic with which he is totally unfamiliar. He needs to spend some time learning about the topic, and then deciding what measurements he needs to add the relevant data. Then, the role of the statistical consultant would be different and a lot more guidance and leadership is expected.
Example. In a study (Esfandiari), the objective was to build a survey to measure ethics of elementary school children who were being taught how to create a business toward entrepreneurship. The first step was to invite a few major entrepreneurs and talk to them about the definition of “who is an entrepreneur” and what consists of ethical behavior in the field of entrepreneurship. After one-on-one interviews with these individuals and reading the relevant theory, it was decided that surveys would be useless for assessing the attitude of elementary school children toward entrepreneurship. Consequently, it was decided to create a survey that consisted of small case studies to which elementary school children could identify. For example, one item sounded as follows.
Mary sits at the same table with two more students, Jane and Deborah. Mary likes red pens but he does not have any. Jane has five red pens. Would be OK if Mary takes one of Jane’s red pens.

It would be fine because Jane will still have four red pens.
It would be fine if she asks one of the red pens but lets Jane know
It would not be right for her to take one of the red pens.

What was interesting was that after studying the literature, we found that one always needs to include one ethical, one unethical, and one conditionally ethical items because children who are at a certain age may not yet have developed the relevant schemata, menta structure, to have a full understanding and interpretation of abstract concepts such as ethical behavior.

Discloses conflicts of interest, financial and otherwise, and manages or resolves them according to established policies, regulations, and laws.

One of the major dimensions of EQ that was discussed in the prior chapter is learning about the constructive methods of “conflict resolution”, which is a field of itself in the world of today. We have experienced this multiple times in working with teams of students on the consultation process. We offer an example:
Example. In one of the projects during the pandemic the Asian students were interested in analyzing the president Trump’s tweets calling covid-19, the “Chinese Virus” and the ill effects that it had on the Asian community. Half of the team members were complaining that others had stolen the project and were running with it. The other half were complaining that they are not following anything and nobody answers their questions and they felt excluded.
To solve this issue, we took the following steps:

Talked to each member alone and listened to what they had to say.
Talked with the whole team and asked them to communicate their concerns with the rest.
This created a much more comfortable atmosphere among the members because they all felt they were being heard.

It looked like half of the students on this team were more advanced and were using methods like NLP (natural language processing), and similar techniques to analyze the tweets. Whereas, the other group members were not familiar with such techniques.
After talking to everyone, they decided to divide the task according to their qualifications and interest and it all worked well. However, the major thing was to use the right leadership, hear everyone, and guide them to come up with a decision that worked for the entire team.

Promotes the dignity and fair treatment of all people. Neither engages in nor condones discrimination based on personal characteristics. Respects personal boundaries in interactions and avoids harassment, including sexual harassment, bullying, and other abuses of power or authority.

In the time of post-Covid, one of the projects that the students worked on was a content analysis of Trump’s comments calling it the “Chinese Virus”. One group of Chinese enrolled students decided to take Trump’s tweets on this subject and employ different methodologies such as NLP (Natural Language Processing) and other related methods (Wordcloud, Sentiment Analysis) for finding patterns and development of predictive models of the relevant data. The group consisted of eight members and with two or three weeks to complete the project, half of the members complained that the other half had co-opted the project and they were not being treated as an integral part of the team.
In order to resolve this problem we first spoke to the members individually and tell their story. This needed 15-30 minutes per person, then we brought the entire group together to speak and listen to each other. The conclusion we reached was not one of disrespect or exclusion but instead some were more advanced in their techniques and were too excited about modeling. The others were not well trained enough to follow along and felt excluded.
We came to the conclusion that it would be best to divide the team into two separate groups and have each group work on a project tailored to their own interest. Both groups wound up being quite successful with equally sound results having used different datasets.
The conclusion reached was that in order to promote dignity and inclusion, it is best to invest time in listening to individuals and then to the entire group before making a decision as to what to do.

Acknowledge the conflict existed
Talk to everyone individually and within the group to clarify
During the group meeting, we listened and while some agreed verbally, we had to look beyond their words to learn the truth of the team dynamics.
We tried to work on an action plan that was acceptable to everyone before the groups reconvened.
We sought feedback and agreed on a plan of action. proposing a solution involving common goals and interests stratified by ability
We closely monitored the progress of each group until they finished their projects.

(Suggested reading: Preventing and Managing Team Conflict)

Take appropriate action when aware of deviations from these guidelines by others.

In the consulting course, we require students to give us the following:

R Code
Codebook
Cleaned Data
Final Report

All results must be reproducible, therefore all project work must have a clear workflow from raw data to final product. In this way, deviations can be identified quickly therefore it is not in any team’s interest to deviate from agreed guidelines and rules. In many projects, our clients continue to modify the raw data and desire to re-run the new data using the code provided by the teams.

Acquires and maintains competence through upgrading of skills as needed to maintain a high standard of practice.

In the consulting course, we ascertain that the students are able to integrate the previously acquired knowledge to solve real problems and also introduce additional more advanced methods to continue building their skills for specific clients.
Example. ROC are widely used in the biological sciences to report the accuracy of medical treatments as well as concepts such as false positive, true negative etc. Once students have demonstrated mastery they are better able to help our clients.
We are frequently the recipients of data from survey organizations within the university and outside. For example, student attitudes on current topics or educational interventions on changes in attitude. In our department, we do not offer courses in scale construction, generalizability theory, evaluation of reliability or factor or cluster analysis. We teach these methods in the consulting course encouraging continuing education and upgrading of skills.

Follows applicable policies, regulations, and laws relating to their professional work, unless there is a compelling ethical justification to do otherwise.

We train the students on the ethical practice of statistics and data science through having them read and analyze the relevant literature, view videos that demonstrate ethical practices in different disciplines, AI, medicine, law and business.
We further ascertain that the students have mastered these issues in ethical practice through team projects which teach/discuss ethical practice and the violation of them. Student teams then create videos which are used to demonstrate their mastery.
We have sought the permission of teams who created videos on “Ethical practice of data science in different disciplines” to use their videos as teaching tools. The link to some of these videos are given below:

Upholds, respects, and promotes these guidelines. Those who teach, train, or mentor in statistical practice have a special obligation to promote behavior that is consistent with these guidelines.

We promote and teach the ethical guidelines of ASA though the following channels

We require the students to read, analyze, and discuss the ethical guidelines of ASA on their own and as a team
As checking for understanding we have teams of students: 1) analyze case studies in which ethical guidelines of ASA have been breached, 2) identify the unethical practices that occurred, and 3) propose a solution for correcting the unethical practice of data science in the context discussed.
As homework we have teams: 1) read, analyze, and summarize the guidelines of the ASA on their own and as a team, 2) write case studies in which some of the Ethical Guidelines of ASA are breached, watch videos of studies in which guidelines of ASA have been breached and answer questions about instances in which unethical practice of data science has happened.

Example 1) Guidelines given to students and their teams for study:

This goal is served best when a competent statistical practitioner is included early in the research design, preferably in the planning stage.*
Ethical Guideline II.A.8: Recognize that any frequentist statistical test has a random chance of indicating significance when it is not really present. Running multiple tests on the same data set at the same stage of an analysis increases the chance of obtaining at least one invalid result. Selecting the one “significant” result from a multiplicity of parallel tests poses a grave risk of an incorrect conclusion. Failure to disclose the full extent of tests and their results in such a case would be highly misleading.*

These recommendations may be based on prospective power analysis, the planned precision of the study endpoint(s), or other methods to ensure appropriate scope to either frequentist or Bayesian approaches. Study scope also should take into consideration the feasibility of obtaining research subjects and the value of the data elements to be collected.

title: 1 Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
(Vivian)
Communicates data sources and fitness for use, including data generation and collection processes and known biases. Discloses and manages any conflicts of interest relating to the data sources. Communicates data processing and transformation procedures, including missing data handling.

Is transparent about assumptions made in the execution and interpretation of statistical practices, including methods used, limitations, possible sources of error, and algorithmic biases. Conveys results or applications of statistical practices in ways that are honest and meaningful.

Example. A student team was examining the results of a surgery where a small percentage failed. This is a situation where a contingency is unbalanced and did not meet the assumption of standard logistic regression and instead moved a rare events method (logistf in R). In terms of algorithmic bias, the patient database used for the training of the models is predominant white and male and results may not generalize beyond this population. The student team conveyed their warnings and assumptions clearly to the client.

Communicates the stated purpose and the intended use of statistical practices. Is transparent regarding a priori versus post hoc objectives and planned versus unplanned statistical practices. Discloses when multiple comparisons are conducted and any relevant adjustments.

Example. A medical doctor is conducting a study to test the effectiveness of wearable technology which monitors blood glucose and blood pressure on the reduction of emergency room visits. The study involves two groups of participants: one receiving this device (treatment group) and the other receiving a similar device without the ability to monitor blood glucose and blood pressure (placebo/control group). He has approached our student consultants for assistance.
The consulting team clearly communicates that the primary objective of the statistical analysis is to compare the difference in emergency room visits between the treatment and control groups.
They intend to use poisson regression to determine if the difference in the count of E is statistically significant.
The A priori objective was to compare the count of ER visits. This was established before the study began. The Post hoc objective was developed after observing the data. The team decided to also compare the interaction between the two groups. This is disclosed as a post hoc analysis since it was not part of the original study design.

Meets obligations to share the data used in the statistical practices (e.g., for peer review and replication) as allowable. Respects expectations of data contributors when using or sharing data. Exercises due caution to protect proprietary and confidential data, including all data that might inappropriately harm data subjects.

Example. In a consulting course designed for statistics and data science majors, teams of students work with a real client. The client provides them with the data set, and the questions to be answered. As the first step of dealing with big data, the team is expected to create subsets of data that include the potential variables that are of interest to the client, think of methods that have the least bias for replacement of missing data, and seek the input of the client with regards to cut off points for changing numerical to categorical data.
This happens a lot in medical consultation. For example, age is a contributing factor to hypertension. Often clients want the odds of hypertension as a function of age range. Student teams cannot decide on intervals that look statistically feasible. For example, they cannot divide age by ten year intervals because the odds of hypertension does not change every ten years. It might not change much till sixty and then have a major spike. Rather, the statistical consultant needs to ask the client about the appropriate cutoff points. Additionally, at the termination of the project, the team is supposed to provide the client with a clean data set, a clear codebook explaining how each variable were measured, and computer codes that allow the client to rerun the results after additional data has been added so that the same results could be reproduced.
Additionally, the statistical consultant should ascertain that the data are de-identified, if needed get IRB (Institutional Review Board) approval. If the statisticians are consulting with an institution, they cannot use their data for teaching or publish the results unless they have permission from the relevant resources. Therefore, it is clear that multiple steps need to be taken to keep the confidentiality and integrity of the data provided by the client.

Strives to promptly correct substantive errors discovered after publication or implementation. As appropriate, disseminates the correction publicly and/or to others relying on the results.

Example. Sometimes when the clients want to publish the results of the work done by student consultants in a refereed journal article, they might come back and ask for additional analysis or explanation of the findings. In that case, the results produced by the student teams are re-examined and reanalyzed if so that the potential mistakes made by the student teams are corrected and the manuscript is ready for publication in a refereed journal. In situations like this the help of another statistical consultant or graduate student who is well versed in the methodology can be sought and generally they are offered co-authorship.

For models and algorithms designed to inform or implement decisions repeatedly, develop and/or implement plans to validate assumptions and assess performance over time, as needed. Considers criteria and mitigation plans for model or algorithm failure and retirement.

Example. In an on-going relationship with a client, the research needs and the underlying data can change. Electronic medical records are such an example. Patient data is continually updated with each visit and possible adverse events. Statistical analyses, dashboards, and models need to be robust and/or modifiable to keep up with the changing needs. A dashboard example might be a predictive model of congestive heart failure as a function of factors such as age, weight, gender, presence of diabetes, etc where the client has the ability to update the data to generate new predictions.

Explores and describes the effect of variation in human characteristics and groups on statistical practice when feasible and relevant.

Example. In a study we wanted to examine the effect of two teaching methods, case-based instruction vs. theory on teaching students, who were trained to be math and science teachers at high school level, how to apply theories of motivation and multiculturalism in actual teaching. The student teachers were randomly assigned to two groups. One group was taught theories of motivation and multiculturalism plus real case studies in which the teachers showed how to apply these principles in everyday teaching.
At the end of the study it was shown that strong students (top 25%) did equally well whether they were taught by theory or case-based instruction. While students that had challenges (bottom 25%), did a lot better with case-based instruction. It is like analyses of cases helped them decipher, and analyze the theoretical principles behind motivation and multiculturalism while learning how to apply it in reality.
In a similar study we showed that case-based instruction helps to close the gap between the performance of statistics and social science majors in applied statistics class.

title: 1 Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
Seeks to establish what stakeholders hope to obtain from any specific project. Strives to obtain sufficient subject-matter knowledge to conduct meaningful and relevant statistical practice.

(NEW)Striving to obtain sufficient subject-matter knowledge to conduct meaningful and relevant statistical practice.

Example one. In the second part of the consulting course, the student teams are assigned to work with real clients from different disciplines including medicine, administration, education, and public health, etc.. Generally speaking the statistics and data science majors are not familiar with subject matter knowledge in medical topics such as diabetes, liver transplantation, eye surgery, and relevant comorbidities.
One of the major messages that we try to give the student teams is that in the majority of consulting projects, the statistical consultant is not familiar with the subject-matter knowledge of the project. Through the consulting project and working with the client the teams learn that they can acquire the relevant subject-matter knowledge by reading the background literature recommended by the client, ascertaining that they have a clear codebook explaining the relevant variables and how they were measured (interval, nominal, ordinal, etc.), making sure which variables the clients want to include in the study, and a good understanding of relevant comorbidities (the simultaneous presence of two or more diseases or medical conditions in a patient) and covariates (variables that are correlated with the outcome and could potentially affect the results). Through reading the background literature, learning about the variables of the study, and communication with the client regarding potential covariates and comorbidities, the teams can make better decisions regarding the application of relevant and meaningful statistical practice.
For example, in the evaluation of an educational intervention on the effect of an after-school program on standardized math scores for middle school students from lower socio-economic-status, the team found a very high correlation between pretest and posttest standardized score on math. When they used the pretest scores as a predictor, they found a very high R-squared. However, when they treated pretest scores as a covariate, or used gain scores (difference between pre and post scores) as the outcome variable, the R-squared shrunk considerably; showing the real effect size of the intervention.
Example two. Another example is medical projects. A team of students worked on a project related to congestive heart failure. After talking to the client and reading the prior literature, they learned that hypertension (oscillations in blood pressure) is a major
predictor of congestive heart failure. Whereas, in another project related to cosmetic eye surgery the team learned that patients with high blood pressure are not at an elevated risk for complications from plastic eye surgery.
In situations where high blood pressure could be a potential risk, the team learned that they can start with a predictive model that includes blood pressure and one that does not. Then, through the comparison of the models in terms of practical significance and seeking the input of the client, they could decide to keep the potential risk factor in the predictive model or exclude it.
Example three. There are studies published in the literature where the accuracy of classification models is reported close to 100%. However, when you analyze the methods of data analysis, you notice that the investigators could have ignored the low frequency of data in four distinct categories, pooling them into a single category, and running a model like logistic regression that would create close to 100% accuracy. However, the correct approach would have been to use logistic regression for rare events or downsample the category with very high frequency.
One of the projects that our students worked on and resulted from Dr Ariana Anderson’s famous study on baby cry is a good example of this situation.
https://www.chatterbaby.org/pages/
Dr Ariana Anderson was the client on this project and had the team go through two steps:
1) Do a literature review in which the investigators tried to maximize the accuracy of their findings by including every possible variable in the model of pooling the categories with very low frequencies that had no resemblance. The students were also required to find some articles in which such wrong practices did not happen and the accuracy was not close to 100%. The objective was to show the good and bad practice of data science.
After reading and classifying the relevant articles into good and bad statistical practice an example of which is presented below. For example, as you notice in the summary reported to the study in row three, the accuracy is ranging from 67% to 100%.


2) Use the multiple data sets provided to them by the client, plus the relevant guideline lines to divide the cries into the relevant categories while deleting junk cries. The following is a sample table and plot created by one of the teams on the relevant types of cry and as it is noticed around 83% (270/326) of the cries in the hungry category. So, adding the cries in all the other categories is going to increase the accuracy of the model provided. However, this would not be practicing good statistics.




Working on this project taught the students that their main objective as an ethical data scientist is not to create a model with the highest accuracy. Rather it is ascertaining that they have sufficient subject-matter knowledge to conduct meaningful and relevant statistical practice; so that the accuracy of the model they provide is reliable, valid, and generalizable.
Having sufficient subject-matter knowledge is key in ethical practice because it is quite easy to go to sources such as Google and ChatGPT to obtain code for running the different statistical methods that we hear about. However, running code without solid knowledge of relevant theory and practice with something as simple as the effect of small number of observations on lack of reliability of the coefficients of the resulting model could create major ethical problems in the application of data science to fields like medicine (using AI to recommend a number of drugs out of 100 drugs), education (recommendation of a certain method of teaching over another), business (deciding the margin of risk for a certain stock), and psychiatry (for example, practice of cognitive science over medication or psychotherapy or combination of medicine plus psychotherapy, etc.) Currently, data science is used to make major decisions by all types of professionals and these decisions affect individuals’ wellbeing in many respects. To that end, knowledge of statistical methods used is a major prerequisite of being an ethical data scientist. As we progress, the knowledge in this field keeps changing and evolving. Thus, it is our responsibility as educators in this field to ascertain that not only our students acquire the skills in coding and data visualization, but alo, they acquire the relevant knowledge and more important than all develop the confidence that they are able to acquire new knowledge on their own or have the attitude to go and learn it through taking on-line classes and other resources that are available in the world on today. This is so because getting an undergraduate or graduate degree in statistics and data science does not give anybody the ticket to assume that they have not acquired all the knowledge they need to be an effective, successful, and ethical data scientist. This is just the beginning of an end.
Below is a sample table of the summary of results provided by one of the teams of baby cry trying to be a bad (unethical) and good (ethical) statistician.

Our conclusion is that from the educational viewpoint it is not sufficient to have the statistics and data science majors just read about the ethical guidelines of ASA, they need to see examples in which these guidelines are breached, critiques such examples, create their own imaginary case studies of unethical practice and if possible engage in projects where they have the chance to practice ethical and unethical statistics. This kind of leaning needs to be experiential and not socratic.
In the above we discussed at length the importance of striving to obtain sufficient subject matter knowledge to conduct meaningful and relevant statistical practice. One of the major components of ethical statistical practice in the world of today is ascertaining that the study data is clean, accurate and correct. This is so because often the data scientist is provided with multiple files that need to be combined, data which needs to be obtained by scraping, textual data, as well as voice or video recording data. Therefore, having the knowledge and skill to obtain and create accurate and correct data is the prerequisite to analysis of data, and reporting the results.
Example 2. In one of the studies conducted, the client required the team to obtain written comments on the teaching performance of the professors from the web and conduct a sentiment analysis of the relevant comments.
Based on the team input, the majority of their time involved scraping the data from the web, and preparing it for analysis. In order to do this, team members had to either acquire or update their data scraping skills. Actually based on the talk by one of our guest speakers, who graduated from our program five years ago, and is now a major data scientist at Meta (formerly Facebook), he said that he spent 60% of his time getting the relevant data ready for analysis.

Example 3. Oddly enough even in the application of AI to a large data set, under-representation of certain groups creates the same kind of problem that we discussed in the baby cry project that involved a small data set; meaning that the coefficient of predictors found on smaller subpopulations have largest standard error and this not the same reliability/consistency as the coefficients for large samples. For example, suppose that we are examining the effect of some medication on lowering congestive heart failure among four different ethnicities with a total sample of 100,000; while only 2% of this sample is from ethnic background four. Consequently the odds that we find for heart failure as a function of ethnic background are not equally reliable for the four ethnic backgrounds.
Therefore, data management skills, as well knowledge of subject matter, and the ability to write the findings within the context of the problem are all necessary skills that are required for successful and ethical practice of statistics.

Seeking to establish what the stakeholder hopes to obtain from the specific project, the following examples can be offered.


Example. The final objective of many consulting projects is to publish the obtained results in a refereed journal relevant to the field of interest . In cases like this the team members take the following steps: 1) make sure to have a clear understanding of the question, the relevant variables, and how they are measured, 2) carry out a detailed EDA (exploratory data analysis) to learn about the type of distributions, needed transformations, outliers, rare events, etc. 3) seek the input of the client regarding transforming the numerical to categorical predictors, and 4) come up with a clean data set with a clear structure that is ready for analysis. Experience has shown that more than 50% of the time of any project is spent on obtaining the subject matter knowledge, and the steps explained above.
Once the final data structure is created, then the team decides on the method of statistical analysis and provides the client with a progress report on the findings. In this meeting the client comments on the findings as well as the additional points that they want clarified. One of the topics that frequently arises in the progress meeting is the inclusion of new and exclusion of some variables that are already in the model.
One of the potential predictors that the clients are often interested in is the interaction or the combined effect of two variables. For example, hypertension, high cholesterol, and smoking are major predictors of congestive heart failure. One of the factors the clients might be interested in is the effect of smoking combined with hypertension on congestive heart failure. Thus, the potential predictors would be the main effects of smoking, hypertension, cholesterol and the interaction between smoking and cholesterol. Generally, if the main effects of smoking and hypertension are significant, the likelihood of an interaction effect that is both practical and statistical significance is relatively low. So, the client might decide to keep the main effects and not include the interaction effect or the other way around.
Explaining and verbalizing interaction effects could become challenging. The most effective strategy would be to present a plot of the interaction effect and interpret it in a manner that is understandable by a non-statistical audience. In a majority of cases with large sample sizes, one could get significant interaction effects whose contribution to the overall strength of the model is negligible. In such instances tha majority of the clients recommend elimination of the interaction effect because while not contributing much to the strength of the model, it makes the model more complicated and less parsimonious.
The last and a very important step of projects whose final goal is publication in a refereed journal is the writing of results within context and in a language that is understandable by the consumers of the research.
In some instances, the team is presented with a data set that requires using statistical methods that they have not learned. For example, in a project involving plastic eye surgery we were faced with an example of “rare events” and the outcome variable was binary. Therefore, we ended up with a data set with an unbalanced number of “1’s” and “0’s”. Therefore, standard logistic regression could not be used to analyze the data and the team had to learn how to conduct logistic regression with rare events. The team was provided with relevant resources to learn conducting and interpreting logistic regression with rare events. This topic has been added to the theoretical part of the course. Another project that required using hierarchical linear modeling was treated similarly.


Regardless of personal or institutional interests or external pressures, does not use statistical practices to mislead any stakeholder.


Example one. In a consulting study (Esfandiari), the objective was to examine the effect of law-related education on the knowledge of the US Constitution and students’ attitudes towards issues such as police and authority, intercultural relations, and community engagement. This study was conducted in six states on 2000 students. This was a quasi-experimental design and the classrooms were randomly assigned to study the regular social studies book plus cased in law-related education while the control group only studied the social studies book.
At the end of the study it was shown that the students in the experimental group showed an average agin in knowledge of US constitution that was statistically and practically significant. Whereas, the average gain in the students’ attitudes towards constructs of interest were statistically significant (due to large sample size) but not practically significant.
I made it clear to the stakeholders that the results of the study could not guarantee further funding for continuation of the study as far as enhancing students’ attitudes towards issues such as police and authority and community engagement was concerned. It was not easy to explain the importance of effect size and power to the stakeholders. But, finally I was able to show them that the average at pretest was pretty high and it did not change much after the intervention.
One problem here could have been the social desirability of the items on the attitude scale so that the students would tend to agree with the positive and disagree with the negative items. Unfortunately, I had no role in designing the survey and by the time I got there all the data had been collected. That is why it is preferable for the statistical consultant to be there from the inception to the completion of the project.
Example two. A second example was when the stakeholder was interested in creating a dashboard that would help the students in enrollment. In many instances some required classes fill up very quickly and students who need to take them as a prerequisite or a graduation requirement cannot enroll. The objective of the project was to have the students use the dashboard to enroll in a similar course that the university would accept as equivalent to the course that is filled out. This dashboard was developed based on the description of the relevant courses in the university catalog and it had a good level of accuracy.


Uses practices appropriate to exploratory and confirmatory phases of a project, differentiating findings from each so the stakeholders can understand and apply the results.


Example one on Exploration. In the consulting class we encounter some clients who come to us with some data that has been collected with an exploratory survey with no specific questions to be answered. An example was a project, funded by a donor, that intended to look at the volunteer experiences of STEM majors. The objective of the study was basically exploration and trying to find out the areas in which students volunteer and describing how the volunteer experience affected them.
Analysis of the quantitative data from the survey showed that most of the volunteer experiences were done in centers close to campus such as hospitals, nursing homes, or tutoring on campus and what they learned from it. Techniques like word cloud and sentiment analysis were employed to analyze the students’ open-ended comments about their volunteer experience. This was an additional door to understanding the dominant effect of volunteering on students’ emotions and attitudes.
In situations like this the type of exploration that is called for would be “exploratory” as the client is not trying to show whether they have reached a certain objective. Rather, the client wants to get an impression about the overall effect of the project.
