Of course the results of the exploration would help the client to design a survey with more structure that would help to answer the donors’ questions.

Example two on confirmation. Our campus has a “Center or Community Engagement”. The overall goal of this center is to create opportunities for UCLA faculty, students, and staff to collaborate with community partners to build an equitable and just society through community-engaged research and teaching and community programs.
Our students in the consulting did two projects for this center both of which could be an example of confirmation.
In the first project we were provided with the overall objectives of the center as well as the syllabi of 14 courses taught by instructors who teach community engagement across campus and in different disciplines. The objective was to confirm the extent to which the goals stated on the instructors’ syllabi were aligned with the goals of the Center for Community Engagement.
In the second project a survey was designed (Esfandiari) aligned with the educational objectives of the center for community engagement. This survey was administered to the students enrolled in civic education courses across campus in week one and week ten of the quarter. The statistical analyses employed were confirmatory. In other words, we were trying to find out if the factor structure that resulted from the students’ responses to the survey were aligned with the overall objectives of the center for community engagement.

Informs stakeholders of the potential limitations on use and re-use of statistical practices in different contexts and offers guidance and alternatives, where appropriate, about scope, cost, and precision considerations that affect the utility of the statistical practice.



problem with repetition of the same statistical method

One of the situations in which ethical practice of statistics can be breached, is frequent use of the same statistical technique. For example, if we wanted to examine the effect of an intervention on multiple outcomes, repeated use of the two-sample test of the mean would increase type I error. We are assuming that all these repeated t-tests are independent of each other. For instance, if we want to examine the effect of an intervention (like a teaching method/or medication) on five different numerical outcomes that could potentially be correlated, the five repeated use of two-sample test of the mean will decrease the confidence level to (0.95)^5 = 0.66; assuming alpha of 0.05. Consequently, the confidence level will decrease to 0.66 and the p-level or the actual risk of rejecting a true null will increase to 0.34. In such a case use of a one factor MANOVA (multivariate analysis of variance) is recommended.
The same dilemma could happen with multiple use of Chi-square to examine the relationship b multiple categorical predictors. This problem would be solved with the use of classification methods such as logistic or multinomial regression, depending on whether the outcome is binary or has more than one level.
Suppose you want to predict the relationship between standardized test scores with high school and college GPA. Repeated use of simple linear regression is another error. This is due to the fact that the predictors are highly likely correlated to each other and we are presented with a problem of multicollinearity so that the sum of R-squared resulting from the sum of two simple linear regression is not equal to the R-squared resulting from multiple linear regression.

Applying the same statistical method to problems of different nature

One can come across situations where the same statistical method such as multiple linear regression is applied to data that are non-linear without making the right transformations. This creates the major problem of meeting the assumption of linearity and therefore, the results not valid and reliable.
Suppose we use a linear regression model to predict test scores from the instructional method. While presenting this model to clients, it is important to inform them that this model is based on this specific data set and assumes a linear relationship. This model may not be accurate for a different school or set of students or if the relationship between the method and the test scores is non-linear. We would also suggest alternative solutions if the stakeholder can foresee changes in the design and collection of data.

Problem of rare events

One of the major problems that we have encountered in students’ projects is that in spite of our emphasis on having reasonable N in different categories, they do not carry out the proper EDA, such as looking at the contingency table of a categorical outcome with categorical predictors and hurry to creating the relevant model. Then the software generates meaningless outputs with coefficients that are hard to interpret and do not mean anything. In such situations students have to be educated to learn how to carry the relevant analysis for rare events, pool categories to create the acceptable sample size, or just do not use the variable that is causing trouble. It has to be emphasized that the small sample size increases error and creates coefficients that are not reliable and stable.

Explains any expected adverse consequences from failing to follow through on an agreed-upon sampling or analytic plan.

A situation that came up in a consulting situation was when we designed an intervention to examine the effect of an after-school program on fifth grade students’ knowledge of economics and their attitude toward entrepreneurship and starting a new business. The design that we agreed upon, to randomly assign half the students enrolled in the after school program to participate in the intervention and the other half to a sports program. The plan was to have both groups pre- and post-tested on their knowledge of economics and attitude toward entrepreneurship and starting a business prior to and after the intervention. Later when I received the data, there was a single group of students all of whom had participated in the intervention. When I asked the school principal, “why did they do it differently?” he responded: “All the parents wanted their children enrolled in the intervention and we could not take that right away from them”. So, now the study had boiled down to a pre-post design and drawing causal conclusions from such a study would not be possible. That is why it is advised to have the statistical consultant be aware of every step of the study including design of instruments, data collection, implementation of intervention, etc. otherwise, one is not going to get the conclusions one planned for.
Another example is a study that I was involved in with one of the major local high schools with 3000 students. The objective of the study was to teach ninth grade students, who were just entering high school, life skills. This high school included close to 20 ninth grade classes. The objective of the life skills class was to help the students who had just entered high school develop higher self-esteem, develop the feeling that they are in charge of their own success or failure, and learn how to solve conflicts with their classmates and others. The dilemma was that all of the high school students had to go through this program. So, the challenge was to create a design that gave us a control group; while all classes went through the class. This problem was resolved by having ten out of twenty classes take the life-skill class in the first semester and the other twenty take it in the second semester. That way the classes that took the class in the second semester served as the control group and yet everyone took the life skills class. This is sort of a causal design in which intact groups are randomly assigned to the intervention. Such designs are referred to as quasi-experimental designs and still enable the investigator to draw causal conclusions.
In addition to pre and post surveying the students on the attributes that the life skills class intended to teach, we also observed every class, took notes on students’ interactions, and spoke to the teachers. These are the valuable lessons that we learned.
Some of the classes were taught by school counselors. These classes were not as successful as classes taught by regular teachers . So, we redesigned the class so that in the second year of intervention, the counselors just visited the classes, talked to the students and advised the students of their availability.
The most successful class that won a major award from the district was taught by a teacher that taught methods like conflict resolution in the context of history and as an assignment had students write short stories about conflicts that happened in their personal life and how they used the relevant techniques taught to solve those issues. This helped us learn that conflict resolution needs a real context and cannot be taught like a prescription of steps to follow.
One of the funniest stories was when my GSR was observing a class where the teacher was going through the steps of conflict resolutions and the students at the end of the class were in a fist fight and shooting paper planes.
That made me even become a great believer of “Case Based Learning” and that is why all through my career as a statistics instructor, I have used data that have resulted from my consulting experiences in the teaching of applied statistics classes. These data provided a real context, I had a story to tell, and it taught the students how to interpret their findings within an interesting context.
That is why we start this book with EQ in data science and in the second volume we will present real case studies of how different statistical methods can be applied by statistics and data science majors to solve practical challenges in different disciplines. We strongly believe that these attributes are complementary and data scientists need to have both EQ as well as knowledge of statistics and programming to succeed in solving the real problems in a variety of settings and contribute to the advancements of the field.

Strives to make new methodological knowledge widely available to provide benefits to society at large. Presents relevant findings, when possible, to advance public knowledge.

One of the major goals of our capstone course is to give back to the UCLA community and the community at large. To that end we collaborate with professors and administrators from different disciplines at UCLA including David Geffen School of Medicine, Jules Stein Eye Institute, Chancellor’s office, Summer Session, Center for Civic Engagement, Neuroscience, and Psychiatry in the pro-bono analysis of their data. Their collaboration with us has been invaluable for our students. These scientists have been very generous with providing us with the data, the questions to be answered, and have met with our students at least three times per quarter to: 1) ascertain that the teams have a clear understanding of the question to be answered, 2) listen to the progress report from teams and advising them how to revise their work, and 3) listen to the final presentations and offer their final recommendations.
However, the work does not stop here. Some of these collaborators want to publish their findings. In a majority of situations the work completed by student teams needs to be amended and further analysis of data is required. In these cases, we help the clients with their needs and sometimes ask the course TA to collaborate as well. These efforts have resulted in joint publications and has helped to show the active role that the Department of Statistics and Data Science plays in giving back to the UCLA community.
One of the visible studies related to one of the WASC reviews that UCLA went through. In this study there were 16 questions asked by the Chancellor’s office. We were provided with the relevant data set. The client, Mr. Kelly Wahl, spent a lot of time with the teams clarifying the data, the variables, how the data how to be merged, as well as clarifying the questions that needed to be answered. Based on his input, this data was very helpful in the UCLA WASC Review.
A recent issue that the UCLA administration has been focusing on is “sense of belonging”. It is over a decade that we were allowed to use the extensive data set that UCLA collected on our campus climate in teaching. After meeting with lawyers and the administration, Mr. Wahl was key in helping us get permission to use this data in teaching. One of the interesting points that he raised was that they had to ascertain that all of the contingency tables had at least five observations in each cell. It was explained to me that if a contingency table had one student from a certain ethnic background who majored in engineering, it might make it possible to find out who that student is; jeopardizing the students’ privacy. This is a good example of teaching about privacy of data that students find very interesting. Whereas, if they read about it as a clause it does not mean much to them. These scenarios are very helpful in helping students learn about these concepts and learn what to do if a similar situation comes up.
The campus climate data set has interesting data on sense of belonging, feeling of prejudice, perception of discrimination, friendliness of our environment, academic self-confidence, ethnicity, sexual orientation, whether students are first generation, if the student plans to leave UCLA, etc. The analysis of this data has helped our students with a better understanding and appreciation of our campus climate. When they find out there is no prejudice based on ethnicity, that makes them feel accepted.

Understands and conforms to confidentiality requirements for data collection, release, and dissemination and any restrictions on its use established by the data provider (to the extent legally required). Protects the use and disclosure of data accordingly. Safeguards privileged information of the employer, client, or funder.

Example one
More that a decade ago we were approached by a telecommunication and mass media company to assist with predicting when it was necessary to send a truck to a location to perform a repair. We were asked to make this prediction, given the demographics of the client, the location, urgency of the problem, and other relevant factors. One of the major issues in this study was confidentiality of the information about the clients of that communication company. They wanted to ascertain that the information they provided us would not in any way identify the personal information of their clients. That is why it took quite a few months of talking to lawyers and doing data manipulation until we could get the data. This was a very successful study and continued for a couple of years.
One of the interesting conversations that we had with the graduate student researcher who got a visible job with industry after finishing his PhD was that during the job interview, what had helped him most was talking about his experience working on this project. This is just one example of how working with real clients, and learning about dilemmas that come up and need to be addressed, are an important and imperative step for training data scientists at all levels from undergraduate to the PhD level.
Example
Over a decade ago, UCLA conducted a study on the investigation of our campus climate. This was a very extensive study that involved 255 questions and 5382 respondents. Due to my interest in this topic, I approached the school and inquired whether I (Esfandiari) could use this data in teaching. They put me in touch with Mr. Kelly Wahl, who was in charge of the study at the time. He met with me and mentioned that first of all we need the permission of UCLA’s lawyers. The second point he mentioned was that they needed to make a contingency table of all the categorical variables and ascertain that none of the cells included less than five observations. The interesting example that he provided me with was: “imagine we are looking at the relationship between major and ethnic background and there is only one student on campus that is majoring in biomedical engineering, then this information would make it possible for that particular student to be identified.”
After almost a year of work on Kelly’s part, I was provided with the de-identified data. This is a very rich data set with a lot of useful information about UCLA students’ perception of academics, respect for diversity, inclusion, exclusion, bias, discrimination, friendliness of our climate, with factors such as ethnic background, sexual orientation, ethnicity, gender, whether student is first generation, internation, etc. It has provided me a rare opportunity for writing my lectures, exams, and homeworks around the reality of our campus. It has also been used extensively by our students in conducting real studies in applied statistics series (101 series), the diversity class (stat 112), and the statistical consulting class (141XP).

Prioritizes both scientific integrity and the principles outlined in these guidelines when interests are in conflict.


Case I
At some point when we served as the directors of the consulting center (Esfandiari, Lew) a client called multiple times and wanted to have a meeting urgently to analyze the data that he had collected on the brain. Finally, we arranged a time and met with him along with the Graduate Student Researchers working at the consulting center. As a routine, we asked him for a sample of his data as well as a code book. He went tangential and was not willing to discuss that idea. Instead, he went into a deep elaboration of how in the experiment he had studied EEG through six channels including frontal, parietal, and temporal, spinal,cerebellum, and occipital .
First of all his technique looked very old because in this day and age they place tens of channels to create an electroencephalogram or EEG.
The second puzzle was that he mentioned money is of no concern and we would be paid for whatever we asked.
The final argument was that his statistician who was in Germany had left and did not have access to him any more. We asked to have the contact information of his statistician, so we could contact him and ask for the relevant information. We never got that information either.
Finally, he brought us some kind of output generated by SPSS and said this is some kind of a repeated measures design that shows the findings. Upon examination of the output, we figured it out, this was some kind of ANCOVA and totally unrelated to what he was talking about.
All of these elaborations and excuses looked like the study did not have any integrity and After a few meetings, it was clear for him that we could not fulfill his objectives. Finally, one day he gave us a call and said: “I know you are very busy, so I have found another statistician who can undertake the job”.
This is a clear example of a client who is asking the statistician to jeopardize every ethical rule including questioning integrity of the data, contact with the person who started the work that you are expected to build upon, and working on a project that does not look real. He was simply asking us to jeopardize the ethical guidelines of ASA to fulfill his objective and he simply needed a seal of approval from a well known institution like UCLA.

Case II

A client contracted with one of the GSRs in the consulting center to analyze some data for him. Then, he sent out the paper for publication and the reviewer suggested looking at the data in a different way. Over a year later, he wrote to us saying : “If you remember you did this analysis and you were paid for it”. I have sent this paper for publication and the reviewer has asked a few questions about the analysis, can you write the answers?
I wrote back saying I did not perform the analysis and in order to answer the questions asked by the editor and conduct the statistical analysis suggested by him, we need to make sure that certain assumptions are met. I contacted the former GSR and she said she could do this with a minimal fee (her old rate). He said: “I have no money and my institution cannot pay for it”. He wrote me about twenty emails and pressured me to write one sentence explaining to the editor why the method used was sufficient and that the method he is suggesting is not feasible. He did not stop until I told him what you are asking me is creating an ethical dilemma. He wrote back at least five times asking for one sentence as a response to the editor. I sent him some material to read about further assumptions that needed to be tested and he continued to ask for the one sentence that would convince the editor. I said what you are asking me to do is unethical. Simply tell the editor, in order to implement the method, you are suggesting certain assumptions that need to be met.
This situation has been going on for over a month with multiple long emails. He even threatened to take us to court. I actually welcomed the idea because we had dozens of emails that would prove that what he was asking us to do was not only breaking the ethical rules of statistical consulting but also jeopardizing the rules of scientific publication.
We have frequently used this situation and similar ones to train the graduate student researchers working in the consulting center about not being obliged or forced by clients to go above and beyond the agreements that were made in the written contract.

title: 1 Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
Data Subjects, or Those Directly Affected by Statistical Practices (Vivian)

Keeps informed about and adheres to applicable rules, approvals, and guidelines for the protection and welfare of human and animal subjects. Knows when work requires ethical review and oversight.

In our work at the University of California, ​​there exist programs which oversee the safety
and welfare of Human subjects in projects in accordance with all applicable federal
regulations, state law and institutional policy. The Office of the Human Research
Protection Program (OHRPP) includes the Institutional Review Boards; the
investigators; the study sponsors; and the Research participants themselves. Within the
OHRRP, the program consists of separate units which oversee three fundamental
activities:

A unit that coordinates and supports the activities responsible for reviewing and monitoring all studies and ensuring informed consent for any work that involves Human Subjects.
An education and training unit responsible for the education of all researchers.
A separate unit that monitors and measures the effectiveness and quality of the human research protections.


While our experience has been limited to institutional review boards within the university, the basic principles hold in other situations. Private companies engaged in research involving human subjects are fully expected to protect the rights, safety, and welfare of human subjects. This may involve internal (employees) or external (consulting) workers.
As consultants then, the expectation is that knowledge of the rules and guidelines set for the protection of human and animal subjects are followed regardless of the exact context.


Makes informed recommendations for sample size and statistical practice methodology to avoid the use of excessive or inadequate numbers of subjects and excessive risk to subjects.

There are several aspects of recommendations:

Sample Size Determination: Sample size involves a balance between having a large enough sample to detect an effect (power), but not excessively large which wastes resources or unnecessarily exposes too many subjects to possible risk.

Example. The best example here is the Gallup poll. When Gallup wants to predict the future elections of politicians, they suffice with samples of 2000 and not higher. The statistical method that relates to choosing or not choosing a political candidate is one sample proportion assuming that the proportion of the population who vote for candidate X is 50% or 0.50. We can use the following formula to estimate the sample size
\(`N\  = \ \frac{Z^{2}*(pq)}{(ME)^{2}}`\)

95% confidence interval for a two sided test, then Z would be 1.96
p and q equal to 0.50
ME (margin of error) is equal to 0.02

Then N would be equal to almost 2400
\(`N\  = \ \frac{{1.96}^{2}*(0.25)}{(0.0004)}`\) = 2401
So, with a margin of error of 2%, we need a sample size of about 2400. Now if we increase the margin of error to 1%, the sample size needed would be
\(`N\  = \ \frac{{1.96}^{2}*(0.25)}{(0.0001)^{}}`\) = 9604
When we cut the margin of error in half the sample becomes four times and therefore, the cost increases a lot. Given the margin of error of 2% is acceptable and provides enough power, it is not necessary to go to 1%.
The same is true for clinical trials involving analyses such as two sample test of the mean; comparing two groups or one-way anova comparing multiple groups. In the beginning phases one can start with a smaller sample and in order to reach the power of interest and a reasonable effect size, the sample can be increased.

Statistical Methodology: Ethical considerations might also affect the choice of statistical methods. For example, if the study involves multiple comparisons, appropriate statistical techniques should be used to control the risk of Type I error (false positives). Failing to do so could lead to incorrect conclusions and potentially harmful consequences if interventions are incorrectly deemed effective. Similarly, using statistical methodologies that handle missing data appropriately is crucial, as poor handling can lead to biased results.

Example One. Suppose that in a study we are comparing the effect of three doses of medication on lowering blood pressure. The outcome variable being drop in blood pressure after a period of two months, and the independent variable being the treatment or the three doses of medication.
The null hypothesis tested here is that the three doses of medicine are equally effective in lowering blood pressure. The methodology to use would be one-way anova and if the F-test were significant, we would conclude that the three doses of medicine differ in their effective and we need to follow the analyses with post-hocs like Tukey HSD, which would make all possible pairwise comparisons (method one vs two, one vs three and two vs three) while keeping the error rate at 5%. The most effective way of reporting the findings to the client would be a plot of the means along with error bars reflecting the mean and standard deviation of each group.
Now suppose that a statistical consultant decides to repeat the two sample test of the mean three times comparing group one with two, one with three, and two with three. He is now making the assumption that these comparisons are independent of each other; which is wrong. Assuming that alpha level is 0.05 for each two-sample test of the mean, if they were independent, the overall error would be (0.05)^3 or 0.15 thus reducing the confidence level to 0.85. This is an example of using the wrong statistical method for comparison of the effectiveness of interventions.
Example two. Suppose that you wanted to examine the effect of three GRE preparation programs on being admitted to a school that ranks in the top 10%. So, in this case the outcome variable is binary which is being admitted vs not being admitted to a school in the top 10%. The predictor is categorical with three levels. The right method would be using logistic regression to compare the odds of being accepted to a school in the top 10% for the three programs. The wrong method would be repeating Chi-square analysis three times. Something similar to what was explained in example one.
Example three. In a consulting study (Esfandiari) we were interested in examining the effects of a program on the attitude of elementary students toward opening their own business and increase in knowledge of relevant economic concepts. This study was done in 50 states across the country. It was noticed that in the case of one of the teachers the increase in knowledge of economics was twice as high. Then everybody was excited and wanted to find out what this teacher was doing differently to cause such a huge improvement in the knowledge of economics. After careful consideration of the data it was noticed that all of the students taught by this teacher were in the gifted tracks. While other teachers had no students or some students from the gifted track. Thus, the strong results were not due to her unique instruction but the outliers she taught. It must be mentioned that the survey designed for collecting this data had been prepared before we got involved. This would be an example of understanding more about the outliers and handling them accordingly.
Example four. Another classical example is when you examine the correlation between X and Y, say shoe size and GPA in middle school for students from three distinct groups, e.g., three ethnic backgrounds. This correlation could range between 0.0 to 0.15 for each group. But, when you combine the data and draw the plot for all of the three groups, the coefficient of correlation could be 0.56 indicating a positive correlation between shoe size and GPA.

For animal studies, seeks to leverage statistical practice to reduce the number of animals used, refine experiments to increase the humane treatment of animals, and replace animal use where possible.

Known as the three R’s the goal is to minimize the number of animals employed and to move to non sentient experimental subjects when possible. Several issues arise in the course of meeting these requirements.
Example 1. Optimal Sample design is critical. Suppose a researcher may only use a maximum of six animals in a study. A crossover study, where each horse serves as its own control at different times or under different conditions can significantly increase the power of the study by working to reduce the variability between subjects. Additionally using repeated measures on the same animals over time can also enhance statistical power. Using horses in multiple tests or measurements results in effectively collecting more data points from the same set of animals.
Example 2. Power calculations generated PRIOR to commencing an experiment. Unfortunately some researchers experiment and collect the data without doing this first. Power calculations would have made it clear whether the study with 6 animals could actually achieve the desired power to detect the effect of interest. Some planning before conducting the experiment involving consideration of alpha, effect size and power will save researchers much grief in the future.
Example 3. Utilizing advanced statistical methods that can handle or are designed specifically for small sample sizes and also repeated measures or crossover designs. Mixed models can efficiently analyze data with these complex structures.

Protects people’s privacy and the confidentiality of data concerning them, whether obtained from the individuals directly, other persons, or existing records. Knows and adheres to applicable rules, consents, and guidelines to protect private information.

Serious scientists, teams, and institutions value the privacy of others and the confidentiality of their data. Rules exist in nearly every arena for this purpose and ignorance does not absolve one of responsibility.
Example 1. Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) are committees established institutions which review, approve, and monitor biomedical and behavioral research involving humans with the mission to protect the rights and well-being of the research subjects. All major universities have them. Rather than viewing IRBs as antagonistic, researchers must understand that having additional “eyes” review research proposals to ensure that university affiliated studies are ethically sound and that risks to study participants are minimized.
Example 2. Suppose one is an independent consultant or a researcher unaffiliated with any university or major institution, does one still have obligations? Absolutely, everyone involved with human subjects has ethical obligations to conduct ethical research. The review mechanisms are different, there are federal, state and local laws and there are professional associations and certifications that require compliance with standards.

Uses data only as permitted by data subjects’ consent, when applicable, or considers their interests and welfare when consent is not required. This includes primary and secondary uses, use of repurposed data, sharing data, and linking data with additional data sets.

In the United States until recently, data could be collected by businesses and institutions to collect personal information without express consent. Protection is broader in places such as the European Union. As nations, states and localities move towards greater oversight and protection of individual data, it is appropriate to take steps now to insure the future accessibility of data.
Data has a life cycle from generation, to collection, to processing, to storage - but at each stage it is still personal data and should be treated as if it were still early in the life cycle. If the data is repurposed, consent should be revisited. The saga of the Havasupai tribe in Arizona illustrates what can happen without protection. Ten years after their initial collection the Havasupai tribe learned that their DNA samples which were originally collected for genetic studies on Type II diabetes were being repurposed in ways that would violate their original consent. The researcher failed to follow proper protocols and regulations and perpetrated permanent harm on the participating community and chilled future research among this and related populations.

Weighs the potential impact of statistical practice on society, groups, and individuals. Recognizes that statistical practice could adversely affect groups or the public perception of groups, including marginalized groups. Considers approaches to minimize negative impacts in applications or in framing results in reporting.

One of the most damaging outcomes of the misuse of the Havasupai tribe data were studies conducted on genetic markers of illness such as schizophrenia and alcoholism. These sensitive issues combined with a disadvantaged population (Havasupai poverty is three times the US average) multiplied the adverse effects of the distribution of their data. Given their cultural and social norm, members of this tribe would never have volunteered consent to examine the genetic basis of these diseases. Yet, it happened and permanently damaged the relationship between this tribe and the

Refrains from collecting or using more data than is necessary. Uses confidential information only when permitted and only to the extent necessary. Seeks to minimize the risk of re-identification when sharing de-identified data or results where there is an expectation of confidentiality. Explains any impact of de-identification on accuracy of results.
To maximize contributions of data subjects, considers how best to use available data sources for exploration, training, testing, validation, or replication as needed for the application. The ethical statistical practitioner appropriately discloses how the data is used for these purposes and any limitations.
Knows the legal limitations on privacy and confidentiality assurances and does not over-promise or assume legal privacy and confidentiality protections where they may not apply.
Understands the provenance of the data—including origins, revisions, and any restrictions on usage—and fitness for use prior to conducting statistical practices.
Does not conduct statistical practice that could reasonably be interpreted by subjects as sanctioning a violation of their rights. Seeks to use statistical practices to promote the just and impartial treatment of all individuals.

title: 1 Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
(BOTH)

Recognizes and respects that other professions may have different ethical standards and obligations. Dissonance in ethics may still arise, even if all members feel they are working toward the same goal. It is essential to have a respectful exchange of views.

In a consulting project, I was asked to evaluate the effect of a reading and math program on helping Hispanic students excel in standardized reading and math scores. I thought that designing a repeated measures design that would require teachers to prepare a test along with the objectives of the program each week, grading those assignments and monitoring the progress of the students would be an effective way of evaluating the effect of the program on enhancement of knowledge of math and reading. . I had proposed to have the students complete a survey that would help us evaluate the change in their attitude and interest towards math and reading. This proposal looked very good to the stakeholders.
However, when we went to the implementation stage, the teachers made it very clear to us that they were already overloaded with what they had to do with preparing for their daily lessons, teaching, grading, dealing with issues related to classroom management etc. Consequently, they had no time to carry out the requirements of the proposed design. The ethical dilemma that we faced was that the proposed design would create an overload for the teachers and also take time away from instruction and the quality of their teaching. Thus, we had to change our design and suffice with pre and post testing the students on some valid measures of math and literacy that our team developed.
Therefore, in spite of the fact that stakeholders, teachers, and the evaluator of the program were all working toward the same goal, we had to change our approach after creating a safe space that allowed the teachers to express their concerns. As a result, we had to come up with an evaluation plan that worked for everyone and yet helped to answer the questions of the stakeholders.

Prioritizes these guidelines for the conduct of statistical practice in cases where ethical guidelines conflict.
Ensures all communications regarding statistical practices are consistent with these guidelines. Promotes transparency in all statistical practices.

While carrying out statistical consulting, one deals with a spectrum of clients ranging from those that know nothing about statistics to quantitative researchers like medical doctors, university professors, etc. who have some knowledge of statistics and are generally looking to publish the result of their work. Regardless of the type of client, the statistical consultant needs to take the following steps to promote transparency of statistical practice:

Ascertain that they have a very clear understanding of the question to be answered and the definition of the relevant technical terms. A statistical consultant will be presented with problems in subjects for which they have no training. For example, the project could be related to medicine, business, economics, public health, engineering etc. Thus, after clear communication about the problem to be solved, the statistical consultant needs to acquire the minimum knowledge that is required to have a good understanding of the client’s problem.
The second step that promotes transparency deals with having a clear understanding of the data set and how it was coded. Sometimes the statistical consultant is presented with an excel sheet of data with unclear labeling of variables as well as unclear description of the different levels of categorical data. There are conditions in which no type of transformation helps with normalizing the data and the data for certain numerical variables continues to be heavily skewed in spite of different transformations. Then the statistician decides that it might be best to cut the data at the median and create a binary variable. In cases like this, the statistical consultant needs to seek the opinion of the client regarding where the cut needs to be made. For example, Such decisions can become critical in the field of medicine and even in industry. For example, based on the medical literature, the lifetime risk for congestive heart failure doubles in those with Blood pressure over 160/100 mm Hg. Therefore, if you are working on a study that aims to examine the risk factors of congestive heart failure and one of the variables given to you is diastolic and systolic blood pressure and you need to change this variable to a binary, then you need to look at the literature as well as getting the input of the doctor with whom you consult. You cannot simply make a histogram of blood pressure and cut it at the median so that you generate a balanced number of 1’s and 0’s.
The third step that promotes transparency is preparation of a report that is aligned with the goals of the client.


Example one. Some projects are simply exploratory in nature and the consumer is simply interested in some clear and colorful plots that are labeled clearly and can explain the trend of an event, the relationship between some variables, the frequency of some events, the odds or probability of some events etc. In cases like this transparency is maximized by a clear exploratory data analysis with clear plots and a short by concise interpretation of the plots.
Example two. There are studies where the client is looking to test a hypothesis and find out whether the data collected confirms the research question of interest. Transparency for this study is different from the type of transparency explained in part a.For example, in a study conducted by Jules Stein Institute at UCLA (Kessler, Esfandiari, Rootman), the objective was to find out whether different angles of light have any effect on the perception of beauty and whether this perception differs for Chinese and Americans raters. This study is a mixed design with the between factor being ethnicity (chinese vs American), a repeated factor (rating the beauty multiple times), a random factor (the photograph chosen), the interaction between the between and within subject design; which was best elaborated with a plot of the means with the time on the X axis and two lines presenting the average perception of beauty at multiple occasions by the Chinese and American raters. In examples like this, the statistical consultant not only needs to present the client with exploratory data analysis, but, also the client needs a summary of the statistical table with p-values associated with the between, within, and interaction effect as well as an effect size that shows the practical significance of the study.
.Example three. Some projects may require preparation of a dashboard

title: 1 Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
the Profession (BOTH) - New

Recognizes that statistical practitioners may have different expertise and experiences, which may lead to divergent judgments about statistical practices and results. Constructive discourse with mutual respect focuses on scientific principles and methodology and not personal attributes.

Example one.
In a consulting project (Esfandiari) I was asked to teach a workshop on power analysis and sample size determination to doctors of veterinary medicine. I was told that the only software they were trained in and wanted to use was SPSS and the majority of the samples they dealt with was very small (like the number of horses). Given the preference and the background of the clients I made the following decisions:

To create a common knowledge of baseline through major concepts underlying power analysis (effect size, type I and II error, and power).
Offered the examples of some simple methods such as paired sample and two sample test, one way-anova, test of correlation, and proportions using sample data sets from the field of veterinary medicine.
After clarification of the major concepts underlying power analysis, I used SPSS to demonstrate running the relevant statistical tests.
Then I introduced a free online software (alpha beta) that allows calculation of power and sample size for different statistical methods.
Discussed the importance of using non-parametric methods for analysis of data resulting from small sample size that do not meet the relevant assumptions of parametric methods.
Divided the audience to teams of two to three and encouraged them to practice the concepts and mathematical underpinnings of the statistical methods, to discuss results, running them on SPSS, interpreting their findings, and using alpha beta software to estimate power and sample size.

One of the major topics that came up is how one decides on the “effect size”. We discussed Cohen’s guidelines for small, medium, and large effect sizes for different statistical methods.2 We also discussed using the guidelines in the theory and prior studies. The suggestions offered by some of the faculty who had more experience were useful in this domain.
After this workshop, I realized that having a constructive discourse with the client was very useful in helping me design the workshop. I learned that they: 1) dealt with small sample sizes, 2) did not use complicated statistical analysis, 3) needed to learn basic statistical concepts related to power analysis and the most typical methods they used in the analysis of their data, 4) repeated measures was an integral part of their work, 5) needed to learn about non-parametric statistics, 5) needed to practice conducting the relevant analyses and interpreting them as a team, and 6) the senior faculty had offered good suggestions in terms of typical effect sizes in different types of study.
Example two (Esfandiari). In a study on program evaluation, I was asked to create a survey for attitude toward conflict resolution for middle school students in a school that involved gang activity. Based on the theory of conflict resolution, one of the wrong strategies to approach conflict resolution is to pretend as if nothing happened with the right approach being approaching the person that you have a conflict with and trying to resolve it through constructive communication and developing a plan to prevent it from happening in the future.
So, one of the items in the scale was
If I have a conflict with another student, I pretend like nothing happened and go my way. The guidelines for scoring this item was as follows:
1 strongly agree
2 agree
3 neither agree nor disagree
4 disagree
5 strongly disagree
As a statistical consultant, I always have respect for the opinions of the client about the surveys I am asked to construct for attitude measurement and to that end, prior to the administration of the survey, I share it with the client; which in this study were school teachers and administrators. The client disagreed with my scoring strategy and said that “Strongly Disagree” should have a score of five and not Strongly Agree”. I was told that in this school the gang members approach the students on their way home or in the school and may bother them or entice them to join gang activity. So, this cannot be resolved by attempting to communicate with the gang members and telling them why they do not want to be involved. The safer method is to pretend as if nothing happened and continue onThe their way. This was very educational for me as an evaluator of interventions and it taught me that with mutual respect for the client, one has to share the survey with the client prior to its administration and seek the client’s input regarding the validity of the items developed.

Helps strengthen, and does not undermine, the work of others through appropriate peer review or consultation. Provides feedback or advice that is impartial, constructive, and objective

Example one:
Sometimes a statistical consultant is hired to pick up the work of another consultant that had to leave the project. In instances like this even though one might not completely agree with the work of the prior consultant. It would be best to spend some time to: 1) understand the goal of the project, 2) get to know the data, 3) try to understand the analyses that the prior consultant did, 4) if possible contact the prior client and ask questions about what is ambiguous, and 5) then move on with whatever methodology that is found to be appropriate.
Example two
The statistician or data scientist could be selected to serve on the editorial board of a journal or review papers that need to be selected for inclusion in professional meetings such as the “American Statistical Association”, or act as discussant. In instances like this, the reviewer is expected to provide useful and constructive feedback that would help the author in improving their manuscript, not be biased, and not make comments that would undermine the work done. The same is true when one attends talks given in seminars. Comments that undermine the work of the speaker and create an uncomfortable environment are destructive, not welcome, and do not help anybody.

Takes full responsibility for their contributions as instructors, mentors, and supervisors of statistical practice by ensuring their best teaching and advising—regardless of an academic or nonacademic setting—to ensure developing practitioners are guided effectively as they learn and grow in their careers.

This is one of our main objectives in the consulting course as well as helping the clients our team members serve.

We make sure students are aware of the importance of EQ in data science and help them appraise themselves on the relevant qualities.
We role model the relevant EQs in working with the students and the clients.
In order to help the students develop the relevant EQs such as “internal locus of control” and attributing their success to their own effort rather than external factors, we ascertain that they have the relevant knowledge of data management and theory to successfully complete the projects assigned to them.
To teach them about “active listening”, “communication”, “empathy” and “conflict resolution”, we role model relevant behaviors in our teaching and communication with the client.

4. Promotes reproducibility and replication, whether results are “significant” or not, by sharing data, methods, and documentation to the extent possible.
This is one of the major objectives of our consulting course. At the end of the project, the teams are required to provide the client with the following:

Clean data set
Codebook listing variables of the study (example: Year in school), variable label (year), and variable type (categorical: 1 = freshman, 2 = sophomore, 3 = junior, 4 = senior, 5 =other)
Annotated R code for creating the outputs, tables, plots, dashboards etc.
Final report including abstract, EDA, question to be answered, variables their definition, and how they were measured, Results and interpretation, conclusions, recommendations, and limitations.

In the case of multiple consulting projects our students have worked with, data keeps on being added to the original database that we were provided with. Therefore, once the client has the R codes results can be reproduced.
In some of the medical projects we have worked on, the original sample size was small, over time more data gets collected and it enables the researcher to include additional variables in the study and expand the questions asked. In cases like this a new team is assigned to work on the data. They are provided with the old report, are required to read and understand the work that was done, and continue with where the clients want them to go. The new teams have generally found the work done by the old teams useful and have learned a lot from it.
The majority of the medical research completed by the teams in the consulting course has been published over time. Generally, the client comes back to us for further analysis, clarification and elaboration of results. We have always helped the client with the assistance of graduate students. These consulting opportunities have given our graduate students credit on more publications.
5. Serves as an ambassador for statistical practice by promoting thoughtful choices about data acquisition, analytic procedures, and data structures among nonpractitioners and students. Instills appreciation for the concepts and methods of statistical practice.
As the instructor of the statistical consulting course (Esfandiari), I have established close working relationships with the UCLA community and the community at large. I have been able to acquire data from different resources such as UCLA Summer Session, UCLA Office of the President, UCLA Division of Physical Sciences, David Geffen School of Medicine, Jules Stein Eye Institute, UCLA Center for Community Engagement, non-profit organizations and school district. Due to these connections, I have been able to…

Acquire data sets from the clients,
Have the clients engage a team of students to answer their research questions
Developed and established the reliability and validity of relevant instruments
Helped the clients determine the extent to which they are meeting research objectives.
Have teams meet with clients at four milestones including:


1) learning about the question to be answered and relevant variables,
2) providing the client with a progress report,
3) making a final presentation to the client, and
4) providing the client with a technical report, clean data, computer codes that can be rerun, and a codebook that explains the variables, how they are labeled, and transformations made such as creating categorical from numerical variables.

The major skill that I emphasize is statistical literacy, meaning explaining the findings to non-statistical clients in a simple language and within context. This skill is of utmost importance and is what the real world expects from the individuals they hire as statisticians and data scientists.
My evaluation of student performance has shown that the students tend to be well prepared in our prior class for data cleaning, coding, data visualization, and data manipulation. However, some of the students find it challenging to connect their theoretical knowledge of statistics with the outputs that they create to answer the client’s question. For example, suppose a study involves both random and fixed predictors. Once the team is advised and finds out what statistical method they need to use, they can find the relevant codes on YouTube videos posted online or from ChatGPT. But, once they create the output, students must be able to explain to the client how the different parts of the output answers the question of interest to the client. They need to understand that with this kind of design they are trying to minimize error by taking random factors into account. So, there would be two sources of error including the regular residual and the error due to the random factor. That is why in this course, I try to remind the students of the underlying theory that they learned in prior classes or teach the theory behind the methodology used in case it has not been covered in prior classes.
I have heard from multiple people who play major roles in hiring data scientists in major organizations such as EY (Ernst and Young) and Google that their major challenge with the new hires is that they are strong in coding and have enthusiasm about learning different software such as Python. However, they are not well trained with respect to explaining the findings of their analysis to the client within context and in simple terms. In order to overcome this problem, in the consulting course, I place a lot of emphasis on making sure that the students developing a deep understanding of why they are using a certain statistical method to answer the client’s question and also have them practice statistical literacy by designing the homeworks and the in-class assessment in which they can only have one cheat sheet around the skills explained above.

title: 1 Final Chapter. Ethics in Statistics and Data Science
text:
Statistical Practice(BOTH)

Ensure appropriate statistical practice that is consistent with these guidelines. Protect the statistical practitioners who comply with these guidelines and advocate for a culture that supports ethical statistical practice.

This is what we do in the capstone course to promote section 1 described above.
With relation to enhancing the EQ aspect of data science, Esfandiari who taught applied statistics and also worked as a statistical consultant in a variety of industry settings including evaluation of interventions, entrepreneurship, science education, medicine, public health, law related education, as well as participation in multiple workshops including “creative leadership”, she came to the conclusion that knowledge of statistics and mastery of languages such as R and Python, were necessary but not sufficient for data scientist to succeed in their respective industries.
Esfandiari attended a seven day workshop on Creative Leadership along with a number of executives of major companies from different parts of the world and different companies etc. that were attending to improve their leadership skills. One of the most interesting aspects of this workshop was that each participant was evaluated on their leadership skills by the person they reported to as well as the individuals who reported to them. The findings indicated that in every single case, the individual’s rating by their boss was higher than their self-ratings and the average ratings by the people who reported to them was always lower than the people who reported to them. For some of the participants this was shocking and they were not happy to hear about it to a point that some would say: “I really do not care how the people who report to me perceive me”. However, through multiple activities in the workshop that involved a lot of teamwork, even those who said they did not care how others perceived them came around and started active collaboration with others.
Based on the input Esfandiari provided, she found this workshop to be very educational for her and it helped her learn a lot about her weak points and helped her improve her communication skills as a statistical consultant as well as an academician. Here are two experiences she shared:

The unique thing about this workshop was that none of the participants knew each other and they would never see each other again. Therefore, when they were asked to evaluate you after a team work, they were very honest and criticized you without any reservation. This is how she behaved in two different team activities.



In the first team activity the team was supposed to discuss a problem and come up with a solution. The team activity was recorded and played back. She was very familiar with the dilemma to be solved, took the leading role, let others participate, and worked with the whole team to come up with a solution.
In the second scenario different teams met in a room and each team was presented with a problem. Esfandiari was arguing that the problem asked does not make much sense and after she came up with the conclusion that this problem cannot be solved, stood in a corner and was not cooperative any more. This was taped and later played so the team could observe their behavior. This is how Esfandiari was criticized by others:


Why did you check out after you through the question is vague or is missing some information and stopped collaborating with others.
Not every problem can be solved right away, you must continue to talk with other team members and discuss what was missing in the question.

Based on input by Esfandiari, this experience was very educational and enlightening for her. As academics, we go into the class well-prepared and we have solutions to the problems that we discuss in class, the problems we design for homework and the projects we assign the students. However, this is not the process in the working world. Many times we are presented with problems where the client may not yet be sure about the exact factors that affect the outcome or could just need help with exploring or unveiling the underlying causes or attributes of a certain phenomena.
Esfandiari mentioned this experience took her back to a course whe thought on creativity. In that course she assigned an article entitled: “The Best Ways to Kill Creativity” by the well known researcher in Yale, (Sternberg). In this article, Sternberg starts with his experience in giving a talk to a group of highly educated people. He says “I presented the group with a problem and said: ‘What do you think is the best solution to this problem?’”. The majority of the people in the group were trying to find a statistical method they had studied or some kind of algorithm to find out how to solve the problem. In other words, a majority were fitting the statistical method to the problem rather than the other way around. This has been one of the unfortunate approaches in education where the major efforts has been to fit the curriculum to the child rather than the child to the curriculum. Sternberg goes on to explain that another best way to kill creativity is the emphasis of the single way to solve a problem. Another well known Swiss scientist and cognitive science Jean Piaget says” :The difference between a scientist with others is the element of “surprise”; meaning that simple things surprise scientists. Scientists get surprised at what they see and hear and are curious to learn about it.
In Stat 141XP, one of the behaviors patterns that is observed (Esfandiari) is that when the client presents the team of students with a data set and a problem to be solved, the first reaction of the team is: “This looks like logistic regression, or we it seems natural language processing might work”. The teams do not have the experience that the most important part of consulting is developing an accurate and thorough understanding of the problem to be solved. This involves a very clear understanding of the operational definition of the outcome and predictor variables as well as how they were measured.
This reminds us of this well known quote: “Someone asked Einstein ‘if you had sixty minutes to solve a problem, how would you spend it?’. He replied: ‘I would spend fifty minutes trying to understand the problem and ten minutes trying to solve it’”. (need to check the exact quote).
The novice students in 141XP do not realize the importance of understanding the exact question of the client. As soon as they are presented with a problem, they want to look in their tool box of statistical knowledge and computer skills to find a solution. Since we expect them to play the role of an experienced statistician and find the answer to the question asked by the client, we need to fill the gap between a novice and an expert.
In Stat 140, we try to fill these gaps by paying attention to the enhancement of the EQ aspect of data science and in Stat 141XP, through having teams work with a real client and solve a real world problem, we provide a situation for the teams not only to practice their EQ skills learned in Stat 140XP, but also, use their knowledge of statistics and data cleaning skills that they enhanced in 140 in working with a real client and solving a real world problem.
Stat 141XP also has strong theoretical components. In this part of the course, major statistical methods that have been used in prior projects as well as methods that are not discussed in other classes but are required to answer the questions asked by the clients are discussed in depth. In the relevant homeworks assigned the teams are required to analyze and criticize prior projects that used similar techniques discussed. Examples are:
In-depth discussion of machine learning methods plus major emphasis on multiple methods of measuring accuracy such as ROC curves and plot of odds which are heavily used in medicine and public health projects but not discussed in statistics.
Many of the clients are interested in development of surveys that include Likert scale type and open-ended questions as well as analysis of them. In our undergraduate curriculum, we do not discuss construction of scales as well as methods for measuring their validity and reliability. This is another theoretical method discussed in 141XP. Many of the projects we have designed for the UCLA community have involved this methodology and it is also used in industry, commerce, marketing etc.
One lecture that the students find very helpful is entitled” “What Statistical Method to Use When”? This lecture follows the following format.










Method


outcome


predictor


typical research questions




multiple regression


numerical



numerical


categorical


categorical*categorical


numerical * categorical


(* implies interaction effect)






Can annual income be predicted from age, gender, and level of education?




Does the effect of gender of income vary with level of education?




Does the effect of gender on annual income vary with years of education?







ANOVA


numerical



categorical


categorical*categorical






Is annual income related to level of education?




Does the effect of level of education (two year college, college, graduate) on annual income vary with gender.







Logistic regression


Binary



numerical


categorical


categorical*categorical


numerical by categorical



Is having diabetic type II related to age, smoking (non-smoker, prior smoker, smoker) and the combined effect of smoking with hypertension as well as combined effect of hypertension and age?




etc












An example project was our work with Center for Civic Engagement. A project was designed to help the center evaluate:

The alignment of the syllabi of courses taught in civic engagement with the overall goals of the center. It has to be pointed out that these courses are taught in multiple disciplines and the major objective is to: 1) help the students acquire knowledge of civics, 2) improve the attitude of the students toward civic engagement, and 3) provide community activities that enables the students to practice civic engagement. In this projects the students were provided with 13 syllabi from different instructor and they did a quantitative as well as qualitative analysis of the syllabi and their alignment with the goals of the center. In this project they were required to use the data visualization skills in Stat 1410 XP, as well as their skills of text mining, bigrams, neuronetwork and sentiment analysis.
In the second part of this project, our objective was to evaluate the extent to which Center for Civic Engagement succeeded in enhancing the students’ knowledge of civics as well as their attitude toward civic engagement. To that end (Esfandiari) developed a scale including Likert Scale items and open-ended questions to reach this objective. The content validity of the scale was established by sharing it with the clients from Center for Civic Engagement, revising it based on their comments, and ascertaining that it has and reflects the goal of the Center. The team first used their knowledge of psychometrics to evaluate and calculate the reliability of the scale and then used quantitative methods to analyze the data from the Likert scale and their knowledge of data manipulation skills from Stat 140 to analyze the open-ended comments of the students.
Another topic are regression and experimental design models that involve both random and fixed effects and require the implementation of hierarchical linear models, random intercept, and random slope models. This is another topic that is not emphasized in our undergraduate curriculum and we teach students about it.

Another method that we have discussed in Generalizability Theory (referred to as “G Theory”). This methodology is not taught in our curriculum either and it is a major approach to reliability analysis in which the different sources of error such as individual, repeated measurement, and the interaction between different factors are taken into account. The different clients, especially those who work with small data and multiple measures (such as eye surgeons), have found this approach very interesting. The teams have used this approach in solving some of the problems presented to us and the results of this type of work have already been published.
Other projects in 141XP rely heavily on the skills that they acquire in 140XP. Examples are projects that require scraping data from the web and creation of data sets that helps to answer the client’s question. An example is a study that involved scraping the UCLA students comments on anonymous professor evaluation posted on the web and coming up with relevant models to answer the client question.
Another example are project that require the construction of webAPPs. An example is as follows: Many times when the students want to enroll in a class, the course is full and they need that class as a prerequisite. In this project, the students were given course descriptions from different departments. The team was told to create a webbAPP that would allow the students to enroll in a similar course when the course of their interest was filled out.
Therefore, in a way the statistical consulting two-sequence course we offer allows our students to engage in upper level thinking. This process involved synthesis of the statistical knowledge and skills they have acquired in their undergraduate education as well as the soft skills they developed in the first part of the consulting course and be trained to operate as a successful data scientist in the world that they will encounter after graduation.
Multiple companies for whom she consulted mentioned that they had to send newly hired statistics and data science majors to workshops in order to enhance their communications and teamwork skills. To that end she proposed a model for helping the students who are enrolled in the capstone course to be exposed to constructs that underlie what is referred to as EQ (emotional intelligence) or soft skills in the world of today. They expressed that these soft skills were essential for successful team work and elaboration of the statistical findings for consumers of statistics. Statistics as a multidisciplinary subject and it is used in every discipline (quantitative and non-quantitative) to demystify the patterns in the small and large data sets collected in different disciplines.
A schematic of this model is presented in chapter one. Once Esfandiari, Lew, and Zes who teach “Introduction to Statistical Consulting” and have also done consulting in various industries, agreed on the structure of the model proposed by Esfandiari, the next step was to write the relevant chapters that introduced the readers to the definition of the underlying constructs, as well as coming up with an approach to measure them.
In the next step (Esfandiari, Lew, and Zes) wrote a number of chapters along with exercises (workshops), that provide the readers with the opportunity to learn about the definition of the constructs underlying EQ in data science. Additionally, after a thorough search of the literature and relevant media, they created surveys in the form of Likert scales.
The dimensions of the underlying model include “active listening”, “communication”, “conflict resolution”, “empathy”, “flexibility”, “locus of control”, and “team building”. These concepts are discussed in chapters two to seven.
In Statistics 140XP, students are initially instructed to practice communication skills and listening skills in two formats (a) individually, by recording a personal introduction and revising it for the purpose of sharing with others and (b) in pairs through a randomized meet and greet exercise (see the appendix/Chapter for the exercise). At the completion of this set of exercises and evaluations, students begin initial forays into team building via a set of exercises.
Team building in a multicultural setting has its challenges but the process of resolving these are uniquely satisfying to our students. We start by showing students a successful end result - a team presenting findings to a client and fielding a set of clarifying questions from the client in a follow-up. From that, we build out towards that end goal along the way working through skills such as active listening, flexibility, empathy.
As an illustration, approximately midway through the term, students are randomized into teams and asked to design a data dashboard with the goal of communicating the relationships in the data to a client with minimal computer literacy (e.g., able to use a portable device or desktop computer with a high level interface such as Windows). The end result is presented and open to critique by their peers and supervising faculty. Successful teams demonstrate that they are able to work as a cohesive unit and develop a dashboard from initial sketches to working model in the specified time frame. These teams have also practiced communication, listening, empathy, flexibility and conflict resolution in the process.
Based on Esfandiari’s extensive experience teaching applied statistics, writing about applied statistics, and consulting in the evaluation of program/interventions in industry, education, entrepreneurship, medicine, and public health, etc. as well as her participation in a seven day workshop on “Creative Leadership” that involved answering major battery of scales that related to creative leadership , she proposed a model that elaborates the underlying constructs that help to introduce and educate the students with respective to those components of emotional intelligence that are needed to succeed as a data scientist or anybody who aspires to succeed in working in a collaborative and yet competitive environment.
After discussing this model with (Lew, and Zes) who teach Stat 140XP, we all agreed on the relevant components and the next step was how we should go about evaluating the extent to which our students have developed this attribute. The best model that we could follow was a pre and post design. Ethically speaking we needed to give all the students to take the survey. Therefore, we could not follow a model like random assignment of some students to take the survey and others did not take it.
In the next step, based on the review of the relevant literature, we developed the relevant scales and after careful revision of the scales developed, an online survey was created. The items on the survey are in the form of a Likert scale (one to five). However, in order to allow the students to choose answers that could reflect decimals (like 3.5, or 4.4 etc.), Zes developed an algorithm that made this possible.
This model is discussed in chapter one of this book and it involves six components including active listening , communication, empathy, developing an internal locus of control (learning to associate their success and failure to their own efforts), conflict resolution, empathy, and team building. With respect to data management and visualization, we teach the techniques of creating clean and correct data, and creation of visualization tools such as dashboards, web APPs, and other related skills.
In order to evaluate the extent to which the students have improved in the EQ aspect of the course,we (Esfandiari, Lew, and Zes) have developed a bank of surveys that measure the attitude of the students toward the six mentioned components and created an online survey that the students take prior to registration is Stat 140XP and after finishing 141XP. Sample results are provided in the appendix.
In addition to the pre and post data on the six components of EQ, the survey also contains many questions about the demographic background of the students. In the future courses, we will be able to share the findings with the students enrolled in these classes and also use this data in teaching the theoretical part of 141XP. Research has shown that the students find the data that is about them very interesting. Therefore, we plan to use this data as a teaching tool.
IN 141XP:
We teach the knowledge of new statistical methods that are not discussed in other courses and have them practice writing the results found within context and with plots that are understandable by a non-statistical audience. These topics are selected based on the projects that have been presented to us by the clients over the last two decades. Example topics are: random intercept models, generalizability theory, creation of valid and reliable instruments for attitude measurement, and in-depth analysis of classification methods used in medical research including concepts like ROC curves which we do not discuss in our courses.
Through the activities elaborated above, we ascertain that our students are trained to engage in appropriate statistical practice and comply with these guidelines and advocate for a culture that supports ethical statistical practice .
Promote a respectful, safe, and productive work environment. Encourage constructive engagement to improve statistical practice.
In both Stats 140 and 141XP, a safe and comfortable working environment is created for the teams to work on the projects assigned to them. Due to their training in the EQ of data science, the teams have been collaborative and generated professional reports as well as presentations to the client. Conflicts have arisen on rare occasions between team members. But, due to their training in constructive techniques of conflict management, the team members have learned how to resolve their disagreements such as one member contributing more to the project or not including everyone in group emails, or few members taking the active role and leaving others out, or some members not showing up to the team meetings by talking to each other, or bringing it up with the instructors.
Once we are presented with a situation like this our strategy with dealing it is as follows: 1) we talk to each team member individually, 2) we have a meeting with the whole team and allow each member to explain their position, and 3) we have them agree on a strategy that is agreeable with everyone.
In rare circumstances, we have had to split large teams into two because of different interests and different skills in programming and knowledge of statistics.
For example( Esfandiari)in a project one team was really interested in analyzing president Trump’s comments on calling the corona virus and “Chinese Virus”. This was during the time of the pandemic when teaching was online and it was hard to find actual clients to work with student teams. Half of the group members were very excited about the project and quickly scraped all the verbal comments by Presenting Trump from the Internet and started using methods like NLP (natural language processing) as well as mathematical modeling of the relevant data. This part of the team was complaining that the other members are not paying attention and participating in the work. Whereas, the other half were complaining that they are not included and do not feel they belong to this team. After talking to all members individually and talking to them as a whole group (Esfandiari), it was concluded that the members who felt excluded just did not have the same skill set as those that were using NLP and mathematical modeling of the data. While in the group meeting they were verbalizing that they will try to collaborate and make this work, it was concluded that this collaboration could not be very constructive due to the difference in knowledge, interest, and skill set of the two parts of the team. Thus, it was concluded to allow those that had actively worked on the project move forward with their part and the other half of the team chose another project to work on.
