They were given some extra time to finish and both teams ended up creating very thorough and comprehensive final reports.
The conclusion that we can reach from experiences such as the above is that training in EQ and in skills like conflict resolution does help the teams to settle their disagreements in most of the situations. However, in some circumstances, the instructor needs to intervene by: 1) listening to each team member, 2) having the whole team meet and allowing each one to express their viewpoint, and 3) coming up with a solution to resolve the dilemmas at hand in a way that is best for everyone. In some circumstances it might help to ask another colleague to attend the final meeting in order to get another viewpoint.

Identify and/or create opportunities for team members/mentees to develop professionally and maintain their proficiency.

In 141XP (Esfandiari) we stop lecturing on Statistics in week five. However, attendance is required in both lecture and section and the teams meet to work on their projecst. This strategy has been very successful and the team members do not need to find time outside class to work on the project. Thus, they will have 15-20 hours in which they can meet with each other to work on the project while the instructor meets with each group, monitors the progress of their work, and answer any questions they might, The work on the final project is completed using the milestones elaborated below:
Milestone one: Develop a clear and concise understanding of the client’s question
The teams meet with the client in week five. The client presents the team with the data set, the codebook, and question to be answered. If possible, this meeting is held in person and if not then using Zoom because some of our clients are not local. The meetingt lasts about 30 minutes to one hour. During that time, we try to ascertain that the team has a comprehensive and accurate understanding of the problem to be solved. We do this by: 1) Making sure that the team has a full understanding of the predictors and the outcome involved in the question asked. 2) Identify how the variables are measured. For example, which variables are numerical, which ones are categorical with how many levels as well as the definition of each level. If a numerical variable has been transformed to categorical, they need to know how it was done, why it was done the way it was, and what each level means. For example, BMI (Body Mass Index) is a numerical variable. In medical data this variable can be treated as categorical (low BMI <18.5), (average BM 18.5 -25), (BMI indicating being overweight 25-30), and (BMI indicating obesity>30). Once the team has a thorough understanding of the question to be asked, we have them re-write it in their own words and read it back to the client. The reason that we spend so much time on milestone one is because experience has shown us that if the teams do not have an accurate and complete understanding of the question and the relevant variables, this would become a major source of error and bias throughout the whole process.
Milestone two. During week eight, the team presents the client with …

A schematic or road map like the following example which shows the relationship between the outcome of interest as well as the predictors. This has proved to be very helpful in helping the team see the big picture and create a foundation for EDA and the next steps in the final report.

In the following example, (Esfandiari), we are using a schematic from an article3 to show a categorical outcome with four levels including no depression or chronic pain. The other three levels include depression, chronic pain or both. There are four hypotheses that also clarify the predictors are of interest:

Is low childhood socio-economic status related to depression, pain or both in adulthood.
Is maternal depression related to the depression, pain or both in adulthood.
Is adulthood socio-economic status related to the depression, pain or both in adulthood.
Does the effect of low childhood SES on adulthood depression, pain, or both vary with age (Interaction effect)


2. A comprehensive Exploratory Data Analysis

A complete EDA (Exploratory data analysis) which involves histogram, frequency tables, contingency tables, side-by-side box plots, correlation matrices, etc. (Esfandiari) Experience has shown that EDA is the most important step in a project, It is during this step that we discover if histograms are skewed and need transformation, this is when we come across situation that none of the transformations used help to create normality in the numerical variables. The next thing we might think of is cutting the numerical variable into categories.
As statisticians we tend to create the cuts that look best from the statistical point of view such as cutting at the median or every quartile to create similar frequencies in the different categories. While after talking to the client, you are instructed that the cut needs to be made at a specific point. This happens very often in medical research where the client would tell you the cut needs to be at a specific number like 4.1 millimeter or a blood pressure of 180/90. This has been very educational for the teams because they learn the importance of communication and involving the client when they are making transformation to the variables.
Another important thing that has happened very often is creation of contingency tables to show the relationship between categorical variables. In many circumstances, the frequency tables generated do not have similar or close numbers in the different cells. Therefore, the application of standard machine learning methods such as logistic for multinomial regression leads to range results in P-value. This is a result of very low cell frequency and the assumptions of the model are not met. In such circumstances one should either employ methodologies designed for rare events or utilize non-parametric statistical methods.
Non-parametric statistical methods are not part of our undergraduate curriculum. However, since they frequently arise in studies where data is hard to collect, we discuss them. For example, experience has shown us that sometimes three different hospitals must collaborate to create a data set that shows a rare surgery for 40 patients. These experiences have taught the teams the importance of EDA and data cleaning. Based on the talk by one of our prior students with five years of experience in the industry, he mentioned that I spent 60% of my time on data cleaning and doing EDA.
These experiences have taught our students that the worst practice for a data scientist would be to try to match a statistical method to the problem presented and start running the model and creating the outcome. What happens is that they end up creating lengthy outputs which they do not know how to interpret.
For example, on the research on areas like campus climate and diversity when I have asked the students about their ethnic background, and suppose we get ten different answers. Another question is whether our campus climate is very comfortable, comfortable, somewhat comfortable, uncomfortable, or very uncomfortable. Suppose the research question of interest is:
Does the effect of comfort in our climate on the sense of belonging to our campus (numerical) vary with ethnicity?
The answer requires us to study the interaction between ethnicity and comfort level. This would create a ten by five contingency table with varying frequencies and sparsity in some cells. Once the regression model is run, it will create many meaningless interaction effects with strange p-values. Then the challenge is: What does this weird model mean and how do I interpret it?
However, EDA would help to pool together certain categories on campus climate and ethnicity and a contingency table with reasonable frequencies and then the created model would make sense, the coefficients would have higher reliability/consistency, and the data would be interpretable. Exposure to such practices helps the teams experience first hand the importance of the reasonable number of observations on the reliability, and interpretability of the coefficients of the model as well as its reproducibility.

3. Decision about what method they are going to use to analyze the data, a sample printout with relevant plots, as well as the interpretation of relevant findings within context.
The third step (Esfandiari) in milestone two is to decide what statistical method the team is going to use to analyze the data to answer the client’s question. In this step the team presents the clients with a number of professionally prepared tables and plots that shows the EDA performed.
The teams are advised not to cut and paste computer output from R or Python to their presentation to the client and the final report.. Rather, they are trained to present their findings in tables and plots presented in the refereed journal articles that are used as a major component of teaching the theoretical part of 141XP. (Esfandiari). These articles are also used as a means of enhancing statistical literacy and teaching the students how to write professional abstracts, publishable articles, with major emphasis on interpretation of statistical findings, tables, and plots within the context of the problem to be solved.
For example, if they are using logistic regression to analyze their data, they are required to create a table representing odds, 95% confidence interval for the odds, plot of odds, R curve, and other indices of accuracy such as confusion matrix, etc.
4. Q and A between the team and the client
In the fourth and final step of milestone two: 1) The client will ask their questions about the results that were presented, 2) suggest the revisions that are needed and the additional analyses or plots that they think needs to be created to answer their questions, 3) the team members are also given a chance to ask clients about any questions they might have or any specific points they are confused about. The typical questions that the teams have generally relates to coding of the data, missing data and if they use imputation techniques, definition of variables, levels of categorical variables, how they could potentially recode or relevel the categorical variables, if they could cut the numerical variables to categories and at what points these cuts should happen . These types of questions come up more often in projects where the team is unfamiliar with the content. Best examples are medical projects. In instances like this the teams are asked to check the definition of unfamiliar terms in the literature and learn about them. Lack of familiarity with the content of the question to be answered is very important. In many instances data scientists and statisticians consult on areas in which they have no familiarity.
In my own personal experience on consulting (Esfandiari), I ended up consulting with a major foundation whose mission was designing and evaluation of interventions that taught students from elementary to college level how to write a business plan and create their own business.
One of the major components of this program was teaching basic knowledge of economic and entrepreneurship concepts. The other component was ascertaining that the intervention helped the students develop a positive attitude about entrepreneurship, developed the relevant skills, and learned about the ethics of business and ethical behaviors that an entrepreneur is expected to exhibit.
My training in experimental design gave me the expertise to design the relevant interventions in the schools. However, I needed to acquire the relevant knowledge and skills that required me to develop the relevant tests and attitude scales that were necessary for the evaluation of the interventions. This is what happens in Stat 141XP at a much smaller scale. The teams have clients from all walks of life including eye surgery, internal medicine, analysis of vocal data, analysis of data resulting from psychiatric scales, civic education, psychology etc. The experience of working with a client and in content areas with which they are unfamiliar helps to prepare them for the jobs that they will get after they graduate from college. They will end up working in business, movie industry, music, sports, non-profit organizations, biostatistics, etc. Thus, they realize that having a thorough understanding of the client’s question requires familiarity and knowledge of the relevant context.
One of my interesting experiences (Esfandiari) was trying to create a scale for measuring ethical behavior in elementary school students. After a major search of the literature and talking to some experts, we concluded that elementary school children would have a very hard time responding to a Likert Scale on business. The other thing that I learned from the search of literature on ethics was that it is kind of difficult to make ethical behavior “black” and “white” and it could potentially depend on the situation. I remember reading about this scenario in which somebody’s loved one had cancer and the medicine cost 2000 dollars and they had no money. One of the options was to let the individual die and the other was to steal money for the drug.
After extensive research and given the age of elementary children who are not yet 14 and based on theories of cognitive science (Jean Piaget), cannot yet engage in “abstract thinking” or “hypothetical deductive thinking”, I decided to present the students with simple scenarios to which they related and created three types of responses including ethical, partially ethical, and unethical. One sample scenario was as follows:
Janice has five red pens, and her friend Mary is unhappy because she has no red pens. What do you think her friend should do because she needs a red pen to complete her drawings.

Borrow one of the red pens and return it so Janice would not even notice it.
Take one because Janice will still be left with four which is more than enough
Tell her parents that she needs a red pen and ask them to get her one

This type of scale resonated with elementary students because the scenarios were simple and they described situations with which they were familiar.
The conclusion that we want to reach is that statistics and data science is a multidisciplinary subject and it is used in every field to answer the questions of interest. Therefore, the individuals trained in this area not only should have the readiness to learn new computer languages and visualization skills, they should also be aware that familiarity with the knowledge of the area in which they are consulting is a necessary step in their success as a statistical consultant, advisor, or practitioner.

Continued communication and collaboration with the client throughout the whole project

Of course the interaction between the client and the teams is ongoing and usually either the client or somebody in his/her team will be available to answer our questions from the inception to the completion of the project. For example, in our projects with Jules Stein (Esfandiari), the professor who has been one of our main clients ( Daniel Rootman, MD, MPH), assigns one of his fellows to each project.
Fellows are individuals who have finished their MD and residency training in their relevant specialization, in this case ophthalmology, and are working for a year or more with a research professor such as Dr. Rootman and their major objective is to collaborate on ongoing research projects, and help with publishing of the research results in the refereed journal articles in their fields.
Fellows are in contact with us throughout the project and answer any question we might have about data cleaning, outliers, definition of medical terms, advising on cutoff points for changing numerical to categorical variables in case of dealing with very skewed distributions, whether the teams are on the right track with the type of analysis they are conducting etc. Having access to these experts makes our collaboration very smooth and also shows the team members the importance of collaboration and communication throughout the different phases of the project.
Another example of our collaboration (Esfandiari) is the work we do with the Chancellor’s Office at UCLA. The Executive Director of the Chancellor’s Office of Data Analytics Office of Academic Planning and Budget. The Executive Director of this office is our own PhD graduate, Dr. Adam Sugano and his colleague Dr. Cecilia Zhang, Associate Director of Data Science, have done an incredible job of supporting the 141XP class by providing the teams with extensive data sets, codebooks, as well as advising them on many consulting projects that relate to data collected on UCLA students, faculty, course work, and how we can improve things. Through collaboration with graduates of our own department the students can get to see the importance of continued contact and collaboration with colleagues and the importance of “giving back to the community”. (Esfandiari).
Examples of sample projects include development of dashboards that help the students in the process of enrollment. Sometimes a required course that the students want to enroll in gets filled up in their department. The objective of the developed dashboard is to help them find a similar course from another department that fulfills the requirement and is accepted by their home department. These dashboards were developed based on the course description provided to the students. The outcome was quite acceptable with reasonable accuracy.
Another project done by our teams (Esfandiari) was a student created ranking of the US colleges and universities and comparing their rankings with the rankings created by US News and World Reports. The findings indicated that the rankings were very similar for the top and bottom ten percent but different for the middle 80%. One of the goals of the project was to evaluate the criteria of what is considered the best college. For instance, one criteria might be that the best college is the most selective. While this may not be the best criteria. The best criteria may be the college that helps students from different backgrounds succeed and graduate.
All of the studies done for Dr Sugano and Zhang involve complicated methods such as web scraping, combining data from different resources, cleaning the data, preparing an appropriate codebook, extensive exploratory data analysis, and using the proper machine learning techniques for doing the relevant predictions and forecasting. The teams probably spend 60% of their time on preparation of the data sets and these are the scales that they are taught in Stat 140XP by Lew and Zes.
Statistics 140 and 141XP complement each other and the set of skills and attitudes that the students acquire in 140XP (which we emphasize EQ in Data Science) is a major prerequisite to the success of a team in working with real clients and solving real world problems in Stat 141XP.
Milestone three. In week ten, the team provides the client with the final an oral presentation of the findings covering the following topics:

Abstract
Schematic of the question to be answered
Statement of the problem
Variables and their definitions and how they were measured
EDA
Statistical method used
Outputs and table
Interpretation of findings
Conclusions
Limitations and recommendations

During the final presentation, the client comments on the definition of variables, EDA, statistical tables, plots, and interpretation of findings. They suggest what revisions need to be made and what additional analysis or tables need to be included.
Later the team incorporates the client’s suggestions and recommendations and prepares the final report.
One of the most useful parts of the final reports are suggestions for further steps that need to be taken. This is very educational both from the teaching and project point of view in 141XP (Esfandiari). These reports are used as teaching tools. During lecture or as homework the students analyze these reports, get familiar with the limitations, and are required to come up with suggestions for methods that could be used to answer further questions that the client wants answered but the prior team had no time to complete.
This is an important step because it creates some kind of continuity in our collaboration with the client (Esfandiari). When we go back to talk to the client about giving our teams new projects, the client is highly likely to want to continue working on some of the prior projects. This particularly happens with very small data sets. I recall (Esfandiari) that in one project related to examining the effect of a new drug on some kind of eye disorder there were four hospitals that collaborated on this study. As the study progressed the four hospitals were able to add to the data set and therefore teams were able to use machine learning methods creating models with more accuracy and less problem of overfitting.
Based on my extensive experience (Esfandiari) overfitting seems to be one of the major problems that medical interventions related to rare disease face. The sample size is very small and yet there are many demographic variables and comorbidities (the simultaneous presence of multiple medical conditions that relate to the outcome of interest) that the researcher is interested in. Therefore, we are faced with too many predictors and a small sample size. This creates not only overfitting but poor practice of statistics.

Final product presented to the client in week eleven includes

Technical final report
Clean data
Codebook
Annotated programming codes


Advocate for appropriate, timely, inclusion and participation of statistical practitioners as contributors/collaborators. Promote appropriate recognition of the contributions of statistical practitioners, including authorship if applicable.

In Stat 140XP the students are prepared for participation as statistical practitioners. This is done at follows:
With regards to the EQ aspect of the course, based on the model proposed in chapter one, in 140XP, we have teams work on projects that involve active listening, communication, flexibility, accountability, empathy, locus of control, and conflict resolution. With respect to “hard” skills, we prepare them for data management and visualization by having teams work on projects involving data cleaning, exploration, and construction of dashboards so they can practice summarizing and presenting data to a non-statistical audience. We work on their presentation skills by having them prepare videos and performing live presentations as a team.
When students enroll in 141XP, they have already been trained on the EQ aspect of their statistical training. In Stat 141XP, we start with the topic of Ethics in Statistics. Have students read extensive literature and watch media resources on the importance of ethics in data science in general and while it is applied to different disciplines. Students are required to …

Read and analyze the Ethical Guidelines for Statistical Practice by the American Statistical Association
Write case studies that exemplify how these ethical guidelines might be broken and jeopardized
Observe and watch YouTube videos related to ethical practice of statistics (see appendix for examples)
Prepare videos on the ethical practice of statistics in multiple disciplines as well as big data statistical methods such as AI and Machine learning.
The chapter on ethics includes the link to some of the videos prepared by the students


Establish a culture that values validation of assumptions and assessment of model/algorithm performance over time and across relevant subgroups, as needed. Communicate with relevant stakeholders regarding model or algorithm maintenance, failure, or actual or proposed modifications.

In 1410 and 141XP we continue to communicate with the stakeholder and our clients through multiple strategies explained below:
Some of the clients we collaborate with such as local school districts, the Office of the UCLA President, Online student evaluation tools such as UCLA BruinLearn, and medical practitioners, maintain databases that continue to grow and have additional data added over time.
The teams who work on managing, cleaning and updating these data in Stat 140XP, as well as the team in 141XP that develop the relevant algorithms for analysis of the numerical as well as written comment, provide the client with extensive codes from different software such as R and Python that enables the client to add to the data based and redo the analysis of their interest. The clients can use these codes to rerun the analyses of their interest after new data has been added to the database.
We continue to work with our clients in multiple stages until they reach their conclusion of interest. For example, in 141XP (Esfandiari) in her collaboration with Center for Civic Education followed the following steps:

Teams were given the syllabi in multiple courses taught on civics and the objective was to analyze the extent to which these syllabi were aligned with the stated Center goals.
In the second step (Esfandiari) developed a survey based on the goals of the center and this survey was administered to a group of students enrolled in this survey prior to after taking the civics class. This involved an in-depth analysis of the quantitative and qualitative analysis of the data. Students used techniques like word clouds, sentiment analysis, and bigram analysis that the clients found very informative and innovative.
In the third stage we were able to collect more extensive data and the client was very happy with the results found. We were told that the results will be used in some kind of review with UCLA as a major participant..

Knows the definitions of and procedures relating to misconduct in their institutional setting. Seeks to clarify facts and intent before alleging misconduct by others. Recognizes that differences of opinion and honest error do not constitute unethical behavior.
Avoids condoning or appearing to condone statistical, scientific, or professional misconduct. Encourages other practitioners to avoid misconduct or the appearance of misconduct.
Does not make allegations that are poorly founded or intended to intimidate. Recognizes such allegations as potential ethics violations.
Lodges complaints of misconduct discreetly and to the relevant institutional body.


Does not act on allegations of misconduct without appropriate institutional referral, including those allegations originating from social media accounts or email listservs.


Insists upon a transparent and fair process to adjudicate claims of misconduct. Maintains confidentiality when participating in an investigation. Discloses the investigation results honestly to appropriate parties and stakeholders once they are available.
Refuses to publicly question or discredit the reputation of a person based on a specific accusation of misconduct while due process continues to unfold.
Following an investigation of misconduct, supports the efforts of all parties involved to resume their careers in as normal a manner as possible, consistent with the outcome of the investigation.
Avoids and acts to discourage retaliation against or damage to the employability of those who responsibly call attention to possible misconduct.

End of Chapter Exercises
Part I Let us examine the following guidelines from American Statistical Association and analyze them
The fact that a procedure is automated does not ensure its correctness or appropriateness; it is
also necessary to understand the theory, data, and methods used in each statistical study. This
goal is served best when a competent statistical practitioner is included early in the research
design, preferably in the planning stage.
Provide an example
Recognize that any frequentist statistical test has a random chance of indicating significance
when it is not really present. Running multiple tests on the same data set at the same stage of an
analysis increases the chance of obtaining at least one invalid result. Selecting the one
“significant” result from a multiplicity of parallel tests poses a grave risk of an incorrect
conclusion. Failure to disclose the full extent of tests and their results in such a case would be
highly misleading.
Provide an example
Clearly and fully report the steps taken to guard validity. Address the suitability of the analytic
methods and their inherent assumptions relative to the circumstances of the specific study.
Identify the computer routines used to implement the analytic methods.
How would you establish the validity of a for assessing depression
Report the limits of statistical inference of the study and possible sources of error. For example,
disclose any significant failure to follow through fully on an agreed sampling or analytic plan
and explain any resulting adverse consequences.
Provide an example
Avoid the use of excessive or inadequate numbers of research subjects by making informed
recommendations for study size. These recommendations may be based on prospective power
analysis, the planned precision of the study endpoint(s), or other methods to ensure appropriate
scope to either frequentist or Bayesian approaches. Study scope also should take into
consideration the feasibility of obtaining research subjects and the value of the data elements to
be collected.
Provide an example
Recognize that differences of opinion and honest error do not constitute misconduct; they
warrant discussion, but not accusation. Questionable scientific practices may or may not
constitute misconduct, depending on their nature and the definition of misconduct used.
Provide an example
Part II Let us analyze the following cases and discuss the ethical dilemma
Case one. A client had one of the GSRs in the consulting center analyze some data for him over
a year ago. Then, he sent out the paper for publication and the reviewer suggested looking at the
data in a different way. He wrote to me saying : “ If you remember you did this analysis and you
were paid for it”. I have sent this paper for publication and the reviewer has asked a few
questions about the analysis, can you write the answers?
I wrote back saying I did not do the analysis and in order to answer the questions asked by the
editor and conduct the statistical analysis suggested by him, we need to make sure that certain
assumptions are met. I contacted the old GSR and she said she could do this with a minimal fee
(her old rate). He said: “ I have no money and my institution cannot pay for it”. He wrote me
about twenty emails and putting me under pressure to write one sentence explaining to the editor
why the method used was sufficient and that the method he is suggesting is not feasible. He did
not stop until I told him what you are asking me is creating an ethical dilemma. He wrote back at
least five times asking for one sentence as a response to the editor. I sent him some material to
read about further assumptions that needed to be tested and he continued to ask for the one
sentence that would convince the editor. I said what you are asking me to do is unethical.
Simply tell the editor, in order to implement the method you are suggesting certain assumptions
need to be met.
This situation has been going on for over a month with multiple long emails over the break.
Identify the problem and analyze the situation.

need to concentrate in order to do well. The second group is told that the test is not difficult and
they should all be able to do well.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
After years of academic work in statistics, measurement, and cognitive science—particularly in learning, development, assessment, and standardized testing—I transitioned into statistical consulting. This shift was not merely a change in professional setting; it was a change in what effective practice required.
My training in statistical theory and methodological rigor provided a strong foundation for consulting across diverse domains, including medicine, education, entrepreneurship, public health, and industry. Yet, as my consulting work expanded, a consistent pattern became increasingly clear: technical expertise alone was not sufficient. A data scientist or statistical consultant may be highly skilled in modeling, inference, and computation, but still fail to produce real-world impact—especially when navigating real clients, real constraints, and high-stakes decisions.
Over time, I came to understand that effective applied work requires a broader set of competencies. Beyond theoretical knowledge and programming proficiency, success depends on interpersonal and interpretive capacities: listening carefully, communicating clearly, managing conflict, and explaining statistical findings in ways that are meaningful to non-technical stakeholders. Equally essential is the ability to recognize ethical dilemmas, resist inappropriate pressures, and uphold integrity when results are inconvenient, misunderstood, or contested.
This book grew out of that realization. It is grounded in lived experience—both successes and difficult lessons—that made the role of emotional intelligence in data science concrete and unavoidable.





Early in my consulting work, I often approached client meetings with a familiar academic mindset: define the problem quickly, identify an appropriate method, and move toward a solution. I soon learned that this impulse—however well-intentioned—was frequently counterproductive.
In consulting, the central task is not simply selecting a technique; it is correctly understanding the problem. Consultants often work outside their subject-matter expertise. When collaborating with surgeons, entrepreneurs, educators, or industry professionals, I had to devote substantial time to learning domain language and practical constraints shaping the research question. I learned that a consultant cannot assume shared terminology, shared assumptions, or shared definitions of success.
One practice became essential: after listening carefully and clarifying key terms and relationships, I would restate the problem in writing and return it to the client for confirmation before any analysis began. This habit—simple in appearance—often determined whether a project proceeded productively or became misdirected.






NoteLesson Learned



A successful data scientist is not only a technical solver but also a translator of human questions into statistical questions—and that translation begins with active listening.





A second lesson emerged while evaluating a life-skills program in a large high school serving approximately 4,000 students. One program objective was to teach ninth graders “conflict resolution.” The curriculum consisted of a pamphlet outlining steps and strategies for managing conflict, which instructors were expected to teach.
Classroom observations revealed that identical content produced markedly different outcomes depending on how it was delivered. In one classroom, the teacher reviewed the steps in a procedural manner while students remained disengaged or disruptive. In another classroom, a teacher introduced conflict resolution through historical contexts and reflective journaling, asking students to connect the steps to conflicts in their own lives. The contrast was striking: when instruction was embedded in meaning and context, students engaged; when presented abstractly, the concept remained detached and ineffective.
This reinforced a principle that applies directly to data science: findings and recommendations must be communicated in context to be understood and used. An analysis that is technically correct but not interpretable or actionable for stakeholders is functionally incomplete.






TipLesson Learned



Data science is not only computation; it is meaning-making. Statistical conclusions must be communicated in accessible language and grounded in the realities of the decision environment.





As teamwork became increasingly emphasized across educational levels—shaped in part by research on cooperative learning—I observed a recurring misconception: many students interpret “teamwork” as dividing tasks, working independently, and assembling results at the end. This approach often produces fragmentation rather than collaboration.
Research on cooperative learning highlights the importance of shared understanding, mutual accountability, and structured coordination. Effective teams require communication norms, conflict-resolution strategies, and collaborative leadership—not simply division of labor.






TipLesson Learned



Collaboration is not automatic. Team success requires interpersonal competence, particularly under ambiguity and pressure, which are characteristic of real-world analytics.





Another formative example occurred while evaluating a program in communities affected by substantial gang activity. The program aimed to strengthen students’ internal locus of control and to develop conflict-resolution skills. I was asked to develop a survey to measure these constructs.
After reviewing the literature (in an era prior to tools such as ChatGPT), I drafted items aligned with theory and prepared a scoring key. Before administration, I shared the instrument with teachers who understood the students’ lived context. One item read:
“If I get into a conflict with somebody, I pretend like nothing happened.”
In classic conflict-resolution frameworks, endorsement might be interpreted as avoidance and scored as undesirable. However, teachers immediately challenged this interpretation. In their setting, students were often advised to avoid confrontation for safety—to walk away and “pretend nothing happened.” The theoretically “correct” scoring did not map cleanly onto practical reality.
This moment underscored a central principle: measurement is not merely technical; it is contextual. Without genuine dialogue with practitioners—and humility about one’s own content knowledge—an evaluator can produce an instrument that is theoretically coherent yet practically invalid.






TipLesson Learned



Strong practice requires continuous communication with clients and serious attention to context, culture, and meaning. Valid measurement depends on shared interpretation.






Over the years, I repeatedly heard a striking comment from industry professionals:
“Universities don’t train students to operate in the real world. We have to send them to workshops to learn communication, collaboration, and leadership.”
This concern is not universally true, but it reflects a persistent gap that emerges when technical training is treated as complete preparation. Students deserve more than learning essential workplace competencies through trial-and-error, anxiety, and avoidable setbacks.
This book is therefore also a response to professional responsibility. We train students to participate in consequential decisions across medicine, law, education, finance, and public policy. Technical proficiency is necessary—but not sufficient—for confident, ethical, and effective practice.



Consulting made the centrality of ethics impossible to ignore. In my own graduate training in statistics, formal education in professional ethics was limited—despite the fact that fields such as medicine and law treat ethics as foundational.
In contemporary practice, data-based decisions shape high-stakes outcomes. Ethical reasoning is not an optional add-on; it is part of competent professional practice. Consulting also exposes analysts to pressures—sometimes subtle, sometimes explicit—to distort interpretations, privilege statistical significance over substantive meaning, or frame results to support predetermined agendas.
One experience from approximately twenty-five years ago remains vivid. I was evaluating a large-scale program with thousands of student participants. Program leadership was focused on achieving statistical significance because funding decisions depended on it. With a large sample, nearly all results reached statistical significance. However, when it became clear that observed changes were not practically meaningful, the response shifted from celebration to frustration.
When I raised concerns about ceiling effects, social desirability bias, and measurement limitations, the conversation became difficult. In effect, the desire for “significant results” had become stronger than the desire to understand reality. I ultimately stepped away from the project. That experience reinforced the importance of preparing students not only to “do the right thing,” but also to anticipate ethical pressure and navigate the professional consequences of maintaining integrity.






TipLesson Learned



Ethical competence is part of technical competence. A well-rounded data scientist must be prepared to recognize pressure, defend integrity, and communicate truth responsibly.





These experiences—combined with my background in cognitive science, learning, and measurement—led me to propose a model for preparing data scientists for real-world success. The model emphasizes five domains:

Theoretical Knowledge of Statistics
Programming Skills
Communication and Team-Building

active listening
empathy
conflict resolution
shared accountability in collaboration

Statistical Literacy

explaining statistical findings in context for non-technical audiences

Ethical Practice of Data Science

This framework has shaped my teaching as well. More than a decade ago, we began offering a four-unit undergraduate course at UCLA titled The Practice of Statistical Consulting. Over time, enrollment grew substantially (recently exceeding 300 students in a single academic year), and the course was revised repeatedly to reflect what students need when they move from classroom exercises to authentic client-based work. Eventually, the course evolved into a two-course sequence: one emphasizing foundational interpersonal and professional competencies, and a second emphasizing the theoretical and practical demands of real consulting projects.
This book extends that work. Its purpose is to make explicit what is often left implicit: the strongest data scientists integrate intellectual rigor with interpersonal intelligence, contextual understanding, and ethical judgment.



One of the most persistent challenges I have observed in teaching is that many students do not perceive the conceptual continuity between mathematical statistics and applied statistics. A familiar example is analysis of variance (ANOVA). Students often experience ANOVA as a collection of formulas rather than as a coherent framework for modeling variation. As a result, they may not recognize that ANOVA and multiple linear regression are instances of the same general linear model, differing primarily in how predictors are encoded—ANOVA emphasizing categorical predictors (and their interactions), and regression accommodating continuous predictors as well.
This disconnect becomes especially apparent when students are asked to interpret variance decomposition. For example, when asked to quantify the proportion of variance attributable to a factor, many do not immediately recognize the conceptual simplicity of the ratio \(`SS_{\text{factor}}/SS_{\text{total}}`\). The mathematical objects are present in their coursework, but the interpretive bridge to meaning, inference, and decision-making is often underdeveloped.
From the perspective of cognitive science, this gap is unsurprising. Knowledge becomes durable and transferable when it is meaningfully connected to prior knowledge. Without explicit opportunities for synthesis—linking established theoretical structures to new applications—students may learn procedures in isolation and struggle to generalize their understanding to unfamiliar contexts.
In my experience, the consulting classroom is one of the most effective environments for cultivating this synthesis because it requires students to solve authentic problems for real stakeholders. In that setting, concepts that were previously compartmentalized across courses must be integrated into a coherent analytic strategy.
Consider a common modeling scenario: a student includes a categorical predictor with sixteen levels (e.g., ethnic background) in a regression model predicting perceived academic success. The model produces fifteen coefficients, several with large standard errors due to small subgroup sample sizes. Yet students may not begin with a simple frequency table or ask whether the data support such a complex parameterization. Despite prior instruction in exploratory analysis and modeling, they do not always apply those habits under real constraints such as sparse data.
More broadly, many students have limited experience reasoning through questions such as:

What happens to the stability and interpretability of estimates when sample size is small relative to model complexity?
How should modeling strategies change under rare events or severe class imbalance?
Should an interaction term remain if it explains only a marginal proportion of variance?
What constitutes a “good” \(`R^{2}`\)in the social sciences, and why does it differ across domains?
Why is \(`R^{2}`\)not an appropriate measure of accuracy in classification problems?

Without authentic practice, students can default to pattern-matching—replicating code, following cookbook rules, and producing output without developing an integrated analytic approach. Consulting disrupts this tendency by requiring students to unify theoretical knowledge, applied reasoning, communication, collaboration, and ethical judgment.
Finally, I have observed an important assessment-related pattern. Team projects often receive high scores, and appropriately so: effective teamwork can yield strong deliverables. However, one-on-one meetings sometimes reveal substantial variation in statistical understanding across team members. With the growing availability of automated code generation and online tools, students can assemble analyses that appear sophisticated without fully understanding the underlying theory.
This observation has shaped my own teaching practices. After years of assigning take-home exams, I returned to closed-book examinations with a single student-prepared reference sheet. This approach better distinguishes group performance from individual mastery, makes conceptual gaps visible, and helps students identify specific areas requiring deeper development.






TipLesson Learned



Durable statistical understanding requires explicit, repeated opportunities to synthesize theory with application in authentic, constraint-driven contexts.








TipLesson Learned



Assessment should separate polished collaborative outputs from individual conceptual mastery to reveal—and remediate—hidden gaps in understanding.





Taken together, these observations point to a central premise of this book: technical competence is necessary, but often insufficient for effective practice. What students frequently lack is not exposure to additional methods, but sustained opportunities to integrate statistical theory with applied reasoning, professional judgment, and clear communication under real-world constraints. The consulting classroom makes that integration unavoidable. It requires students to articulate assumptions, evaluate tradeoffs, confront uncertainty, and translate results into decisions that matter to stakeholders who may not share the language of statistical formalism.
For this reason, I treat emotional intelligence not as an accessory to data science, but as part of its professional infrastructure. The ability to listen carefully, ask precise questions, navigate disagreement, communicate limitations, and act ethically strengthens analytic rigor and increases the likelihood that statistical work leads to sound decisions. The chapters that follow are organized around this integrative view—developing the competencies that connect mathematical foundations to applied impact, and preparing readers to practice data science in a manner that is technically sound, contextually grounded, and broadly intelligible.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
After years of academic work in statistics, measurement, and cognitive science—particularly in learning, development, assessment, and standardized testing—I transitioned into statistical consulting. This shift was not merely a change in professional setting; it was a change in what effective practice required.
My training in statistical theory and methodological rigor provided a strong foundation for consulting across diverse domains, including medicine, education, entrepreneurship, public health, and industry. Yet, as my consulting work expanded, a consistent pattern became increasingly clear: technical expertise alone was not sufficient. A data scientist or statistical consultant may be highly skilled in modeling, inference, and computation, but still fail to produce real-world impact—especially when navigating real clients, real constraints, and high-stakes decisions.
Over time, I came to understand that effective applied work requires a broader set of competencies. Beyond theoretical knowledge and programming proficiency, success depends on interpersonal and interpretive capacities: listening carefully, communicating clearly, managing conflict, and explaining statistical findings in ways that are meaningful to non-technical stakeholders. Equally essential is the ability to recognize ethical dilemmas, resist inappropriate pressures, and uphold integrity when results are inconvenient, misunderstood, or contested.
This book grew out of that realization. It is grounded in lived experience—both successes and difficult lessons—that made the role of emotional intelligence in data science concrete and unavoidable.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
Early in my consulting work, I often approached client meetings with a familiar academic mindset: define the problem quickly, identify an appropriate method, and move toward a solution. I soon learned that this impulse—however well-intentioned—was frequently counterproductive.
In consulting, the central task is not simply selecting a technique; it is correctly understanding the problem. Consultants often work outside their subject-matter expertise. When collaborating with surgeons, entrepreneurs, educators, or industry professionals, I had to devote substantial time to learning domain language and practical constraints shaping the research question. I learned that a consultant cannot assume shared terminology, shared assumptions, or shared definitions of success.
One practice became essential: after listening carefully and clarifying key terms and relationships, I would restate the problem in writing and return it to the client for confirmation before any analysis began. This habit—simple in appearance—often determined whether a project proceeded productively or became misdirected.






NoteLesson Learned



A successful data scientist is not only a technical solver but also a translator of human questions into statistical questions—and that translation begins with active listening.





A second lesson emerged while evaluating a life-skills program in a large high school serving approximately 4,000 students. One program objective was to teach ninth graders “conflict resolution.” The curriculum consisted of a pamphlet outlining steps and strategies for managing conflict, which instructors were expected to teach.
Classroom observations revealed that identical content produced markedly different outcomes depending on how it was delivered. In one classroom, the teacher reviewed the steps in a procedural manner while students remained disengaged or disruptive. In another classroom, a teacher introduced conflict resolution through historical contexts and reflective journaling, asking students to connect the steps to conflicts in their own lives. The contrast was striking: when instruction was embedded in meaning and context, students engaged; when presented abstractly, the concept remained detached and ineffective.
This reinforced a principle that applies directly to data science: findings and recommendations must be communicated in context to be understood and used. An analysis that is technically correct but not interpretable or actionable for stakeholders is functionally incomplete.






TipLesson Learned



Data science is not only computation; it is meaning-making. Statistical conclusions must be communicated in accessible language and grounded in the realities of the decision environment.





As teamwork became increasingly emphasized across educational levels—shaped in part by research on cooperative learning—I observed a recurring misconception: many students interpret “teamwork” as dividing tasks, working independently, and assembling results at the end. This approach often produces fragmentation rather than collaboration.
Research on cooperative learning highlights the importance of shared understanding, mutual accountability, and structured coordination. Effective teams require communication norms, conflict-resolution strategies, and collaborative leadership—not simply division of labor.






TipLesson Learned



Collaboration is not automatic. Team success requires interpersonal competence, particularly under ambiguity and pressure, which are characteristic of real-world analytics.





Another formative example occurred while evaluating a program in communities affected by substantial gang activity. The program aimed to strengthen students’ internal locus of control and to develop conflict-resolution skills. I was asked to develop a survey to measure these constructs.
After reviewing the literature (in an era prior to tools such as ChatGPT), I drafted items aligned with theory and prepared a scoring key. Before administration, I shared the instrument with teachers who understood the students’ lived context. One item read:
“If I get into a conflict with somebody, I pretend like nothing happened.”
In classic conflict-resolution frameworks, endorsement might be interpreted as avoidance and scored as undesirable. However, teachers immediately challenged this interpretation. In their setting, students were often advised to avoid confrontation for safety—to walk away and “pretend nothing happened.” The theoretically “correct” scoring did not map cleanly onto practical reality.
This moment underscored a central principle: measurement is not merely technical; it is contextual. Without genuine dialogue with practitioners—and humility about one’s own content knowledge—an evaluator can produce an instrument that is theoretically coherent yet practically invalid.






TipLesson Learned



Strong practice requires continuous communication with clients and serious attention to context, culture, and meaning. Valid measurement depends on shared interpretation.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
Over the years, I repeatedly heard a striking comment from industry professionals:
“Universities don’t train students to operate in the real world. We have to send them to workshops to learn communication, collaboration, and leadership.”
This concern is not universally true, but it reflects a persistent gap that emerges when technical training is treated as complete preparation. Students deserve more than learning essential workplace competencies through trial-and-error, anxiety, and avoidable setbacks.
This book is therefore also a response to professional responsibility. We train students to participate in consequential decisions across medicine, law, education, finance, and public policy. Technical proficiency is necessary—but not sufficient—for confident, ethical, and effective practice.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
Consulting made the centrality of ethics impossible to ignore. In my own graduate training in statistics, formal education in professional ethics was limited—despite the fact that fields such as medicine and law treat ethics as foundational.
In contemporary practice, data-based decisions shape high-stakes outcomes. Ethical reasoning is not an optional add-on; it is part of competent professional practice. Consulting also exposes analysts to pressures—sometimes subtle, sometimes explicit—to distort interpretations, privilege statistical significance over substantive meaning, or frame results to support predetermined agendas.
One experience from approximately twenty-five years ago remains vivid. I was evaluating a large-scale program with thousands of student participants. Program leadership was focused on achieving statistical significance because funding decisions depended on it. With a large sample, nearly all results reached statistical significance. However, when it became clear that observed changes were not practically meaningful, the response shifted from celebration to frustration.
When I raised concerns about ceiling effects, social desirability bias, and measurement limitations, the conversation became difficult. In effect, the desire for “significant results” had become stronger than the desire to understand reality. I ultimately stepped away from the project. That experience reinforced the importance of preparing students not only to “do the right thing,” but also to anticipate ethical pressure and navigate the professional consequences of maintaining integrity.






TipLesson Learned



Ethical competence is part of technical competence. A well-rounded data scientist must be prepared to recognize pressure, defend integrity, and communicate truth responsibly.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
These experiences—combined with my background in cognitive science, learning, and measurement—led me to propose a model for preparing data scientists for real-world success. The model emphasizes five domains:

Theoretical Knowledge of Statistics
Programming Skills
Communication and Team-Building

active listening
empathy
conflict resolution
shared accountability in collaboration

Statistical Literacy

explaining statistical findings in context for non-technical audiences

Ethical Practice of Data Science

This framework has shaped my teaching as well. More than a decade ago, we began offering a four-unit undergraduate course at UCLA titled The Practice of Statistical Consulting. Over time, enrollment grew substantially (recently exceeding 300 students in a single academic year), and the course was revised repeatedly to reflect what students need when they move from classroom exercises to authentic client-based work. Eventually, the course evolved into a two-course sequence: one emphasizing foundational interpersonal and professional competencies, and a second emphasizing the theoretical and practical demands of real consulting projects.
This book extends that work. Its purpose is to make explicit what is often left implicit: the strongest data scientists integrate intellectual rigor with interpersonal intelligence, contextual understanding, and ethical judgment.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
One of the most persistent challenges I have observed in teaching is that many students do not perceive the conceptual continuity between mathematical statistics and applied statistics. A familiar example is analysis of variance (ANOVA). Students often experience ANOVA as a collection of formulas rather than as a coherent framework for modeling variation. As a result, they may not recognize that ANOVA and multiple linear regression are instances of the same general linear model, differing primarily in how predictors are encoded—ANOVA emphasizing categorical predictors (and their interactions), and regression accommodating continuous predictors as well.
This disconnect becomes especially apparent when students are asked to interpret variance decomposition. For example, when asked to quantify the proportion of variance attributable to a factor, many do not immediately recognize the conceptual simplicity of the ratio \(`SS_{\text{factor}}/SS_{\text{total}}`\). The mathematical objects are present in their coursework, but the interpretive bridge to meaning, inference, and decision-making is often underdeveloped.
From the perspective of cognitive science, this gap is unsurprising. Knowledge becomes durable and transferable when it is meaningfully connected to prior knowledge. Without explicit opportunities for synthesis—linking established theoretical structures to new applications—students may learn procedures in isolation and struggle to generalize their understanding to unfamiliar contexts.
In my experience, the consulting classroom is one of the most effective environments for cultivating this synthesis because it requires students to solve authentic problems for real stakeholders. In that setting, concepts that were previously compartmentalized across courses must be integrated into a coherent analytic strategy.
Consider a common modeling scenario: a student includes a categorical predictor with sixteen levels (e.g., ethnic background) in a regression model predicting perceived academic success. The model produces fifteen coefficients, several with large standard errors due to small subgroup sample sizes. Yet students may not begin with a simple frequency table or ask whether the data support such a complex parameterization. Despite prior instruction in exploratory analysis and modeling, they do not always apply those habits under real constraints such as sparse data.
More broadly, many students have limited experience reasoning through questions such as:

What happens to the stability and interpretability of estimates when sample size is small relative to model complexity?
How should modeling strategies change under rare events or severe class imbalance?
Should an interaction term remain if it explains only a marginal proportion of variance?
What constitutes a “good” \(`R^{2}`\)in the social sciences, and why does it differ across domains?
Why is \(`R^{2}`\)not an appropriate measure of accuracy in classification problems?

Without authentic practice, students can default to pattern-matching—replicating code, following cookbook rules, and producing output without developing an integrated analytic approach. Consulting disrupts this tendency by requiring students to unify theoretical knowledge, applied reasoning, communication, collaboration, and ethical judgment.
Finally, I have observed an important assessment-related pattern. Team projects often receive high scores, and appropriately so: effective teamwork can yield strong deliverables. However, one-on-one meetings sometimes reveal substantial variation in statistical understanding across team members. With the growing availability of automated code generation and online tools, students can assemble analyses that appear sophisticated without fully understanding the underlying theory.
This observation has shaped my own teaching practices. After years of assigning take-home exams, I returned to closed-book examinations with a single student-prepared reference sheet. This approach better distinguishes group performance from individual mastery, makes conceptual gaps visible, and helps students identify specific areas requiring deeper development.






TipLesson Learned

TipLesson Learned



Assessment should separate polished collaborative outputs from individual conceptual mastery to reveal—and remediate—hidden gaps in understanding.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
2.1 Prelude Additions

Audience, Use, Conclusion, and Capstone Exercises**
Book-ready subsections with consistent headers, learning objectives, estimated time, and rubrics.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
2.2 Who This Book Is For
This book is written for readers who already understand (or are learning) the technical foundations of data science and want to become effective, trusted professionals in real-world settings.

2.2.1 Primary audiences

Undergraduate and graduate students in statistics, data science, biostatistics, and related fields
Early-career data scientists, analysts, and statisticians transitioning from coursework to stakeholder-driven work
Experienced practitioners who want to strengthen communication, collaboration, and ethical judgment in high-stakes environments
Instructors designing consulting courses, practicum experiences, capstone courses, or professional-skills modules



2.2.2 Secondary audiences

Project leads, managers, and principal investigators supervising analytics teams
Domain experts (e.g., clinicians, educators, policy staff, product leaders) who collaborate with data scientists and want a shared language for good practice

title: 1 Prelude
section: 2.3 How This Book Can Be Used
text:
2.3 How This Book Can Be Used

2.3.1 For students and self-study

Read each chapter with a “practice lens”: extract one skill, apply it in a real or simulated project, and reflect on what changed.
Build a portfolio: keep your memos, decision briefs, and ethical pre-mortems as evidence of professional readiness.



2.3.2 For courses and workshops

Use the end-of-part capstones as graded deliverables. Each capstone can be completed independently or combined into a single integrated portfolio.
Run short in-class role-plays using the scripts and meeting-note templates to practice conflict resolution and pushback.



2.3.3 For professional teams

Adopt the templates as standard operating practice: problem restatement memo, decision log, decision brief, and documentation checklist.
Use the ethical tools proactively (ethics pre-mortem) and defensively (integrity memo) to protect validity and prevent harm.

Optional implementation: instructors can require a “deliverable bundle” per project milestone (kickoff, midstream findings, final recommendation).

title: 1 Prelude
section: 2.4 Conclusion: Technical Rigor Requires Human Rigor
text:
2.4 Conclusion: Technical Rigor Requires Human Rigor
The central claim of this prelude is straightforward: technical competence is necessary, but often insufficient for effective practice. In real settings, data scientists must translate human questions into statistical questions, communicate results in context, collaborate under ambiguity, and uphold integrity when incentives reward convenience over truth. These capabilities are not accessories to data science; they are part of its professional infrastructure. The chapters that follow therefore treat emotional intelligence, statistical literacy, and ethical judgment as competencies that strengthen analytic rigor—not as “soft” add-ons. A well-rounded data scientist is defined by what their work enables: sound decisions, clear understanding of uncertainty, and responsible action under real constraints.

title: 1 Prelude
section: Shared case scenario (used across all capstones)
text:
Shared case scenario (used across all capstones)
A school district is piloting a new STEM-focused mathematics program for eighth graders across multiple schools. Students are taught in intact classrooms (random assignment is not feasible). Leadership wants evidence to decide whether to scale the program district-wide next year. You are the data scientist/statistical consultant supporting the evaluation. You have a tight timeline, multiple stakeholder groups, and nontrivial measurement and fairness concerns.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
Capstone 1: Theoretical Knowledge of Statistics

Learning objectives

Select an appropriate evaluation design given nonrandom assignment and articulate its assumptions.
Define estimands (what effect is being estimated) and justify unit of analysis and inference strategy.
Identify major threats to validity and propose robustness checks.



Estimated time
90–120 minutes (individual) or 60–90 minutes (team)


Task

Write a 1–2 page Analysis Plan: problem statement, estimand(s), proposed model/design, and key assumptions.
Include at least two validity threats and at least two robustness checks (e.g., sensitivity analysis, subgroup stability, alternative specifications).



Deliverables

Analysis Plan (1–2 pages)
Assumptions and Definitions Log (living table, one page minimum)



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Design choice and justification
Design fits constraints; assumptions are explicit and aligned to the decision context.
Design is reasonable; assumptions are mostly stated but may lack precision.
Design is partially mismatched or weakly justified; assumptions are vague.
Design is inappropriate or unjustified; assumptions missing.


Validity threats and robustness plan
Identifies key threats; proposes concrete, feasible checks that address them.
Identifies major threats; checks are plausible but incomplete.
Mentions threats; checks are generic or weakly connected.
Threats/checks largely absent or irrelevant.


Clarity of estimand and interpretation
Clearly defines what effect is estimated and what it means in practice.
Defines effect with minor ambiguities; interpretation mostly clear.
Effect definition is unclear; interpretation is confusing.
No clear estimand; interpretation not defensible.


Professional structure and readability
Well organized; decision-ready language; concise and coherent.
Generally organized; minor clarity issues.
Hard to follow; excessive jargon or missing structure.
Disorganized; not usable by stakeholders.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
Capstone 2: Programming Skills

Learning objectives

Build a reproducible analysis pipeline with clear data checks and versioning.
Implement diagnostics and produce outputs that can be regenerated end-to-end.
Document decisions in code and README so a peer can rerun the work.



Estimated time
90–150 minutes


Task

Create a minimal, reproducible project structure (folders, scripts/notebooks).
Implement data validation (missingness, ranges, duplicates), a baseline model, and at least one robustness check.
Write a README with exact steps to reproduce results.



Deliverables

Repository or folder with code/notebook + outputs
README (repro steps + dependencies)
Diagnostics checklist (completed)



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Reproducibility
Peer can rerun from scratch with no gaps; versions and dependencies are explicit.
Mostly reproducible; minor missing steps or dependencies.
Partially reproducible; key steps unclear.
Not reproducible; missing files/steps.


Data checks and QA
Comprehensive sanity checks; issues are logged and addressed.
Checks present; some issues not handled or documented.
Minimal checks; likely to miss major issues.
Checks absent.


Code quality and organization
Clear structure; readable functions; comments explain decisions.
Generally readable; some clutter or inconsistent structure.
Difficult to read or navigate; limited documentation.
Unstructured; hard to interpret or reuse.


Diagnostics and robustness
Includes baseline + diagnostics + at least one meaningful robustness check.
Includes baseline and some diagnostics; robustness is limited.
Diagnostics superficial; robustness unclear.
No diagnostics/robustness.

title: 1 Prelude
text:
Capstone 3: Communication and Team-Building

Learning objectives

Demonstrate active listening through accurate paraphrase and confirmation.
Create shared accountability via roles, norms, and conflict protocol.
Document decisions and follow-ups to prevent misalignment.



Estimated time
60–90 minutes (team recommended)


Task

Draft a Problem Restatement Memo to confirm scope, definitions, and success criteria.
Create a Team Charter and a one-page Meeting Notes log using listening loops.
Populate a Decision Log with at least five decisions made during the exercise.



Deliverables

Problem Restatement Memo
Team Charter + Meeting Notes (listening loops)
Decision Log (minimum five entries)



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Active listening and problem translation
Paraphrases are precise; definitions clarified; alignment achieved before analysis.
Paraphrases mostly accurate; some definitions remain vague.
Limited confirmation; translation is incomplete or ambiguous.
No evidence of listening loop or alignment.


Team norms and accountability
Clear roles, cadence, review process, and conflict protocol; shared accountability is operationalized.
Most norms present; accountability plan is reasonable.
Norms incomplete; accountability is assumed rather than designed.
No clear norms or accountability.


Documentation quality
Decisions, owners, and dates are clearly recorded; follow-ups are actionable.
Documentation present with minor gaps.
Documentation inconsistent; owners/dates often missing.
Little to no documentation.


Professional tone and usability
Artifacts are stakeholder-ready and easy to use.
Mostly usable; minor clarity issues.
Hard to use; excessive jargon or missing structure.
Not usable in practice.

title: 1 Prelude
section: Capstone 4: Statistical Literacy (Meaning-Making for Non-Technical Audiences)
text:
Capstone 4: Statistical Literacy (Meaning-Making for Non-Technical Audiences)

Learning objectives

Translate statistical results into decision-relevant meaning and uncertainty.
Distinguish statistical significance from practical significance.
Communicate limitations and boundary conditions clearly.



Estimated time
60–120 minutes


Task

Create a 1-page Decision Brief (or 5-slide equivalent) aimed at non-technical leadership.
Include an Impact Table translating effects into practical terms (and cost of being wrong).
Write boundary statements: what you can and cannot conclude.



Deliverables

Decision Brief (1 page or 5 slides)
Impact Table



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Interpretation and practical meaning
Findings translated into clear practical implications; uncertainty is integrated.
Interpretation mostly clear; some practical implications are underdeveloped.
Interpretation is overly technical or incomplete.
Interpretation is misleading or absent.


Handling of uncertainty and limitations
Uncertainty and limitations are explicit, honest, and decision-relevant.
Limitations stated; uncertainty partially integrated.
Limitations are generic; uncertainty minimized or unclear.
Limitations/uncertainty omitted.


Audience fit and clarity
Language is accessible; structure supports decision-making.
Generally accessible; minor jargon persists.
Too technical or poorly structured for the audience.
Not audience-appropriate.


Decision readiness
Clear recommendation and next steps with tradeoffs.
Recommendation present; tradeoffs partially addressed.
Recommendation vague; next steps unclear.
No actionable recommendation.

title: 1 Prelude
section: Capstone 5: Ethical Practice of Data Science
text:
Capstone 5: Ethical Practice of Data Science

Learning objectives

Anticipate misuse and harm through an ethics pre-mortem.
Respond to pressure ethically and professionally using defensible language.
Document concerns and define escalation pathways.



Estimated time
60–120 minutes


Task

Complete an Ethics Pre-Mortem: identify at least five ways results could be misused or misinterpreted and propose mitigations.
Write an Integrity Memo responding to a scenario where leadership asks you to ‘emphasize significance’ despite weak practical impact.
Draft a Pushback Script and a Documentation & Escalation Checklist for the same scenario.



Deliverables

Ethics Pre-Mortem
Integrity Memo
Pushback Script + Documentation & Escalation Checklist



Rubric










Criteria
Exemplary (3)
Proficient (2)
Developing (1)
Needs work (0)




Risk identification and mitigation
Identifies realistic risks; mitigations are specific and implementable.
Identifies major risks; mitigations are plausible but not fully developed.
Risks are generic; mitigations are vague.
Risks/mitigations absent or superficial.


Integrity under pressure
Response is firm, evidence-based, and professionally framed; protects validity and stakeholders.
Response is principled; may lack clarity or firmness in places.
Response is hesitant or overly adversarial; defensibility unclear.
Response compromises integrity or is unprofessional.


Documentation and escalation readiness
Clear documentation plan; escalation pathway is appropriate and actionable.
Documentation plan present; escalation somewhat unclear.
Documentation incomplete; escalation plan minimal.
No documentation/escalation plan.


Ethical communication
Boundary statements are clear; avoids misleading claims; communicates uncertainty responsibly.
Mostly responsible; minor overclaiming risk.
Mixed messages; boundary statements weak.
Overclaims or invites misuse.
